Long_Description: One of the marks of great science fiction is its ability to portray the future. In Isaac Asimov’s case, he was able to not only to predict it but to actually influence it. His fictional term “robotics” entered the lexicon in our real world, and now describes an entire field of scientific development. The robots he imagined are essentially artificial humans — their “positronic” brains function in a way that’s similar to human brains, and they are able to learn by reading books and change their behaviors over time. In Clarke’s short story collection I, Robot, Asimov poses challenges and puzzles that are still relevant in the world of robotics.

Djinn Chairs, designed by French industrial designer Olivier Mourgue in 1965, are iconic pieces of modernist furniture that became famous largely due to their prominent appearance in Stanley Kubrick’s 1968 science fiction classic, 2001: A Space Odyssey. These chairs are celebrated not only for their distinctive design but also for their role in defining the futuristic aesthetic of the mid-20th century.

The Djinn Chair is a low-slung, lounge chair characterized by its flowing, organic form that appears to hover just above the ground. The chair is made from a tubular steel frame, which is then covered with a thin layer of foam and upholstered in stretch fabric. This gives the Djinn Chair its distinctive, smooth curves and soft, inviting appearance. The chair’s seamless design, with no visible joints or separations, contributes to its futuristic look, evoking a sense of comfort and relaxation while maintaining a sleek, modern profile.

In 2001: A Space Odyssey, the Djinn Chairs were used to furnish the space station and the space hotel, creating an environment that was both futuristic and aesthetically pleasing. Their presence in the film helped to cement the chair’s status as an icon of 1960s design, symbolizing the era’s optimism about the future and the possibilities of space travel. The Djinn Chair’s association with the film has made it a symbol of modernism and forward-thinking design, capturing the imagination of both design enthusiasts and fans of science fiction.

The Djinn Chair’s influence extends beyond its appearance in Kubrick’s film. It represents the ethos of the 1960s, a time when designers were pushing the boundaries of traditional furniture design, exploring new materials, and embracing new forms. The chair is a perfect example of how design can transcend its functional role to become an integral part of cultural history.

Today, the Djinn Chair remains a highly sought-after piece for collectors and a staple in the study of mid-century modern design. Its timeless appeal lies in its ability to evoke a sense of the future, making it as relevant now as it was over half a century ago.

The Djinn chair is a piece of furniture in the Modernist style, created by French designer Olivier Mourgue in 1964. Originally called the "Low fireside chair," it is also commonly referred to as the "2001 chair," because of its prominent appearance in the film, 2001: A Space Odyssey.

The Djinn chair and a chaise lounge, was introduced by Airborne in Merignac Cedex, France. The rest of the series was released in 1965, and includes the iconic "Low fireside chair," a two-seat sofa, and a foot stool. The set remained in production until 1976.

The name "Djinn" refers to an Islamic spirit capable of changing shape. The design's low profile was an attempt to emulate the informal lifestyle of the time. The set was introduced during a time when growing interest in Eastern mysticism was influencing Western decorative arts.
Creator: Olivier Mourgue

Description: 3D Model from the film, 2001: A Space Odyssey, 1968
Long_Description:
The EVA Pod from Stanley Kubrick’s 1968 film 2001: A Space Odyssey is one of the most iconic pieces of technology in science fiction, representing the film’s meticulous attention to detail and visionary depiction of space exploration. The Extra-Vehicular Activity (EVA) Pod is a small, maneuverable spacecraft used by the crew of the Discovery One to conduct repairs, retrieve objects, and perform tasks outside the main spacecraft while in space.

The design of the EVA Pod is sleek and functional, reflecting the film’s overall minimalist aesthetic. The Pod is a compact, spherical craft with a large, transparent viewport at the front, allowing the pilot a clear view of the surrounding space. The Pod is equipped with three mechanical arms extending from the front, each capable of performing delicate tasks, such as gripping tools or manipulating objects. These arms are controlled from within the Pod’s interior, where the pilot operates them using joysticks and controls.

Inside, the EVA Pod is designed for a single occupant. The pilot sits in a chair facing the control panel, surrounded by various switches, buttons, and displays. The interior is snug, emphasizing the Pod’s role as a short-range, task-oriented vehicle rather than a long-duration spacecraft. The controls are designed to be intuitive, allowing the pilot to precisely navigate the Pod in the vacuum of space.

In 2001: A Space Odyssey, the EVA Pod plays a crucial role in several key scenes, most notably when astronaut Dave Bowman (played by Keir Dullea) uses it to retrieve a malfunctioning communications antenna and later when he confronts the sentient computer HAL 9000. The Pod’s isolated, confined environment contrasts sharply with the vastness of space, heightening the tension and sense of vulnerability during these moments.

The EVA Pod symbolizes the advanced technology and the human ingenuity depicted in 2001: A Space Odyssey, but it also serves as a reminder of the fragility of human life in the vast, indifferent expanse of space. Its design has influenced countless other depictions of space vehicles in science fiction, making it a lasting icon of the genre. The EVA Pod represents the intersection of man and machine, a recurring theme in the film, and underscores the challenges and dangers of space exploration.
Creator: Arthur C Clarke, Stanley Kubrick

Description: 3D Model from the film, 2001: A Space Odyssey, 1968
Long_Description: Have you ever asked yourself, what would a vending machine be like if it wanted to kill me? If so, look no further! HAL is capable of many functions, including facial recognition, speech, playing chess, and near-complete omniscience. These traits (some more so than others) have led to HAL representing one of the most sophisticated and frightening villains in science fiction history. Part of what makes his character so effective is that he is a complete enigma, as unknowable as he is competent. His complete lack of personal expression means that there is no way to know what thoughts he might be having beneath his unblinking eye, or how long he has been having them. The only clue offered is in his calm, calculating voice, never wavering even as it apologizes.
Creator: Arthur C Clarke, Stanley Kubrick

Description: 3D Model from the film, 2001: A Space Odyssey, 1968
Long_Description:
The Monolith in Stanley Kubrick’s 1968 film 2001: A Space Odyssey, based on the story by Arthur C. Clarke, is one of the most enigmatic and iconic symbols in the history of cinema. These imposing, black, rectangular structures appear at critical junctures in the film, representing a higher intelligence and serving as catalysts for significant leaps in human evolution.

The Monolith is characterized by its perfect geometric form—tall, slender, and with smooth, featureless surfaces. Standing approximately 11 feet tall, the Monolith’s proportions follow the exact ratio of 1:4:9, which signifies the squares of the first three integers, a detail that suggests deliberate and advanced design. Its jet-black color and minimalist design contrast starkly with the natural environments in which it is found, whether it’s the barren African landscape in the film’s opening sequence or the cold, desolate surface of the Moon.

Throughout 2001: A Space Odyssey, the Monolith is depicted as an object of immense power and mystery. It first appears in the prehistoric past, where it influences a group of early hominins, seemingly bestowing upon them the knowledge and ability to use tools—an event that marks the dawn of human evolution. The Monolith’s sudden appearance and its impact on the hominins suggest that it is an instrument of a higher intelligence, guiding humanity’s development.
The Monolith reappears millions of years later, buried beneath the lunar surface, where it emits a powerful signal aimed at Jupiter. This discovery prompts the mission that forms the central plot of the film, leading to the final encounter with the Monolith in orbit around Jupiter, where astronaut Dave Bowman experiences a transcendent transformation.

The Monolith in 2001: A Space Odyssey represents the unknown and the unknowable, symbolizing the profound mysteries of the universe and the potential for human evolution beyond its current state. Its presence challenges both the characters and the audience to consider the existence of higher intelligence and the vast, unexplored possibilities of the cosmos. The Monolith’s silent, inscrutable nature has made it one of the most enduring and thought-provoking symbols in science fiction, inviting endless interpretation and speculation about its purpose and origins.
Creator: Arthur C Clarke, Stanley Kubrick

Description: 3D Model from the film, Ready Player One
Long_Description: This virtual reality headset is the only way that users can access OASIS, a global online virtual reality platform from the novel Ready Player One. In the story, Wade Watts (or Parzival in the OASIS) lives in 2045, and uses it as an escape from his world. It allows him access to places, information, and ideas that would never be available to him in reality. As VR develops, these fantastic possibilities are opening up to people in the real world, with more and more dreams becoming realized with each passing day.

In Frank Herbert’s Dune series, vast deserts and sand dunes stretch across the planet Arrakis, covering the whole of the planet’s surface and reaching as far as the eye can see. Beneath them lie sandworms. These autotrophic animals are attracted to rhythmic vibrations on the planet’s surface, and it is rare to survive an encounter with one that choose to come up. The only people who regularly do are the Fremen, who revere the sandworms as gods, referring to them as The Great Maker or The Worm Who Is God. They are an essential factor in the creation of Spice Melange, and are almost indestructible. However, their most striking characteristic is their colossal size, as they can grow over 400 meters long. For comparison, adult blue whales on Earth measure in at around 34 meters, meaning the sandworms of Dune are 10 times that size.
Creator: Frank Herbert

Long_Description: Stillsuits are designed for open desert terrains, such as on the planet Arrakis, and are designed to preserve body moisture as much as possible. In this environment, there are no bodies of surface water at all, meaning every drop of water is precious. The primary function of the still suit is to reclaim any moisture that is lost over the course of the day, and to allow it to be reused. Stillsuits are layered, which each layer acting to absorb water through sweating and urination, before filtering its impurities and circulated drinkable water to catchpockets. Assuming it is maintained, a stillsuit can allow its wearer to survive for as long as three weeks in the open desert.
Creator: Frank Herbert

Description: 3D Model from the novel, Snow Crash, 1992
Long_Description: In Neal Stephenson’s novel Snow Crash, VR goggles are used to access an immersive virtual reality realm known as the “metaverse”. This word, which was added to English-language dictionaries in September 2022, has impacted the development of technology in the real world in fascinating ways. Stephenson’s vision of how a virtual reality-based Internet might evolve is as complex as it is intriguing, and the goggles that are used to access this fiction come closer every day to becoming a reality.

Description: 3D Model from the novel, The Diamond Age, 1995
Long_Description: The Young Lady’s Illustrated Primer, from Neal Stephenson’s novel The Diamond Age, is the culmination of revolutionary advances in the field of molecular nanotechnology. The book is made up of smart paper, which adapts and adjusts what it displays based on the reader’s ability and interest. It uses highly sophisticated, interactive technology to personalize stories and encourage its reader’s development. The only primers in existence belong to three young girls: Elizabeth Finkle-McGraw, Fiona Hackworth, and Nell. As each of them interact with and learn from the Young Lady’s Illustrated Primer, it reflects the profound ways that technology can influence each of our lives.

In the two hundred years since Mary Shelley’s Frankenstein has been published, the Creature that it depicts has been represented in hundreds of different ways. As the Creature, known also as Frankenstein’s Monster, grew, it became a living representation of the tension between science and natural laws. It also explores the the intrinsic longing for love and acceptance, the pursuit of knowledge, and the consequences of interfering with powers that are not yet understood. All of these themes are still present in science fiction art and writing to this day, and the influence of one of the most well-known science fiction creations of all time is still felt.
Creator: Mary Shelley

Long_Description:
From: Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

Date: June 3, 2023

Welcome to the Museum of Science Fiction

About a hundred years ago Einstein said, “Imagination is everything. It is the preview of life's coming attractions.”

As a species, humanity is rapidly coming to an inflection point. We now hold the knowledge and the tools
to solve some of our most challenging problems and possibly, in the not so distant future, move towards a
post-scarcity civilization.

At the Museum of Science Fiction, art and science are powered by imagination. This simulation is designed
to preview life's coming attractions - both good and bad.

This project began as experiment to prototype ideas for a physical museum. The technology we used 
allowed us to go beyond experimentation and create a new center of gravity to educate and entertain people. 

Essentially, science fiction is the story of humanity: who we were, who we are, and who we dream to be.
We would like to go beyond 21st century story-telling and provide a space where people can expand
their imagination and consciousness of the real world.

"Reality is that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away" -  Philip k. Dick - Valis, 1981
Here, reality is a persistent environment that can provide inspiration to transcend reality.

We hope you will enjoy exploring the Museum and find inspiration to do something wonderful for humanity.

Greg Viggiano, PhD
Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

Creator: Olivier Mourgue

Long_Description:
This evocative artwork captures a dystopian urban scene, blending mystery and tension. A Humvee is parked in a deserted, narrow alley, its rugged build contrasting with the fragile quiet of the setting. The atmosphere is drenched in an eerie, yellow haze, possibly representing contamination or environmental collapse. In the background, power lines stretch towards distant, shadowy skyscrapers, adding depth to the composition.

A solitary figure in a bright orange hazmat suit stands facing away, creating a sense of isolation and uncertainty. The "Stay Home" biohazard sign on the wall hints at a dangerous, unseen threat, further intensifying the unease. The muted tones of the surrounding environment, paired with the sharp contrast of the hazmat suit, focus attention on the lone figure, evoking questions about survival and the nature of the unseen catastrophe.

This atmospheric piece invites viewers to imagine the world’s story, evoking themes of abandonment, resilience, and fear of the unknown.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
This striking piece of science fiction art portrays a barren, alien world, emphasizing desolation and isolation. In the foreground, a strange rock formation holds what seems to be an ancient fossil, hinting at the planet's forgotten history. A spherical escape pod lies crashed and broken, with dark, jagged protrusions extending from its core, suggesting a violent landing. A lone astronaut is seen emerging from the wreckage, struggling to crawl out into the bleak, unwelcoming terrain. The distant mountains in the background blend into a dusty, almost toxic-looking atmosphere, shrouding the scene in uncertainty.

The muted, earthy color palette of yellows and grays enhances the sense of lifelessness. The tension between advanced technology and ancient, barren landscapes creates a powerful contrast, reflecting themes of survival and exploration in an unknown, hostile environment. The artwork invites viewers to imagine the story behind this catastrophic event, making it both eerie and thought-provoking.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
This haunting artwork portrays a surreal and emotional scene set in a vast, golden field under an ominous, stormy sky. In the foreground, a young girl with braided hair stands in a simple dress, gazing upward. Above her, a disintegrating tank is suspended mid-air, its parts exploding outward in chaotic fragments—barrels, treads, and debris spiraling away.

The stark contrast between the innocence of the child and the destructive machinery creates a powerful visual and emotional tension. The barren field, stretching endlessly around her, emphasizes the isolation and starkness of the moment. The muted, earthy tones of the landscape and the deep grays of the sky further enhance the artwork's somber and foreboding atmosphere.

This piece invites reflection on themes of innocence in the face of destruction, the absurdity of war, and the resilience of humanity amidst chaos. It’s a poignant commentary on conflict and its far-reaching consequences.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
This surreal artwork captures an otherworldly moment in a serene natural setting. Two children, one older and one younger, are depicted running hand in hand across a lush green meadow, their innocent curiosity leading them toward a mysterious phenomenon. Hovering above the ground is a massive, alien-like rock formation, its surface rugged and weathered, defying gravity as it floats in stark contrast to the calm and grounded forest in the background.

The dense line of pine trees and the soft, hazy light filtering through a muted green sky evoke a sense of quiet wonder and suspense. The children’s small figures juxtaposed with the monumental, levitating structure emphasize themes of scale, exploration, and the unknown.

This piece invites viewers to imagine a narrative: Are the children witnesses to a supernatural event, or is this a dreamscape reflecting their boundless imaginations? The scene is enchanting yet subtly unsettling, blending innocence with mystery.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
This mesmerizing artwork features a serene forest scene disrupted by a mysterious, floating metallic sphere. The sphere, perfectly smooth and reflective, exudes a futuristic, otherworldly quality, creating an intriguing juxtaposition with the natural surroundings. Suspended in mid-air among towering, straight pine trees, the sphere seems to radiate a soft glow, subtly reflecting the muted light of the misty atmosphere.

The forest floor, carpeted with yellow-orange foliage, adds warmth and contrast to the composition, while the mist enveloping the scene creates a dreamlike, ethereal ambiance. The symmetry of the trees and the enigmatic presence of the sphere evoke a sense of quiet wonder, prompting viewers to question its origin and purpose.

This artwork explores themes of contrast between the natural and the artificial, the familiar and the unknown. It invites the observer into a moment of contemplation, sparking curiosity and a sense of awe at the unexplained.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
Developed by International Business Machines Corporation in 1971, the IBM 3270 Mainframe Terminal represents a pivotal moment in the history of computing. As businesses and institutions began to rely on mainframe computers for complex calculations and data processing, terminals like the 3270 provided a user-friendly interface, allowing operators to interact with the mainframe remotely.

“Green screen terminals” as they are sometimes called, played a vital role in shaping the way people interacted with computers. By providing a direct link to powerful mainframes, these terminals democratized access to computing resources, fostering innovation across various fields. From business applications to scientific research, the 3270 Terminal paved the way for collaborative computing and laid the groundwork for the interconnected digital world we know today.

The IBM 3270 Mainframe Terminal stands as a testament to the ingenuity of early computing technology. Its intuitive design and robust functionality marked a significant step forward in the evolution of computing interfaces, bridging the gap between humans and machines.
Creator: IBM Corporation

Long_Description:
Introduced in 1972, the SX-70 Polaroid Land Camera marked a paradigm shift in photography. The camera was developed by Polaroid founder Edwin Land, and represents a revolutionary chapter in the history of photography.

Unlike traditional cameras that required film development in a darkroom, the SX-70 allowed photographers to see their images develop right before their eyes by using self-developing film packs. This innovative approach transformed the way people captured and shared moments, making photography more accessible and spontaneous.

The camera’s user-friendly nature and immediate results made it a favorite among artists, documenting life in real-time and capturing candid moments. Additionally, the SX-70's influence can be seen in the development of subsequent instant cameras, paving the way for modern-day instant photography.

Its ability to capture and share instant memories changed forever the way people preserve their experiences. It’s impossible to fathom the countless memories that this camera has immortalized across the globe, and its influence on human creativity and spontaneity in the art of image-making is immeasurable.
Creator: Polaroid Corporation

Long_Description:
This evocative artwork portrays an astronaut collapsed in the golden sands of an alien desert, evoking feelings of isolation and perseverance. The figure, clad in a spacesuit adorned with the insignia of exploration, lies partially submerged in the rippling dunes. The soft, amber hues of the atmosphere suggest an otherworldly twilight, shrouding the scene in a surreal, dreamlike quality.

In the distance, a towering monolithic structure dominates the horizon, its geometric symmetry and mysterious design contrasting starkly with the organic undulations of the landscape. Behind it, an enormous celestial body looms, casting a ghostly glow and reinforcing the extraterrestrial setting.

This piece explores themes of human vulnerability, resilience, and the pursuit of the unknown. It invites viewers to ponder the astronaut's fate and the enigmatic purpose of the distant structure, creating a narrative of discovery and survival against the vast, indifferent backdrop of the universe.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
This evocative artwork depicts a hauntingly desolate landscape bathed in a pale, foggy light. At its center, a derelict train car lies partially buried in debris, its corroded surface emblazoned with the words “Home Sweet Home,” a stark juxtaposition of warmth and decay. A lone figure stands atop the wreckage, gazing into the distance toward a crumbling, futuristic spire rising from the mist. The surrounding ruins of an abandoned structure loom like ghosts of a forgotten civilization.

The muted tones and dense fog amplify the atmosphere of abandonment and mystery, while the figure's solitary stance conveys resilience and introspection. The scene suggests a narrative of survival and adaptation in a post-apocalyptic world, where notions of "home" are redefined amid destruction.

This piece invites reflection on humanity's impermanence and its enduring spirit, urging viewers to contemplate the fragile balance between progress, loss, and the meanings we ascribe to place and belonging.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
This surreal artwork features a solitary figure seated on a simple wooden crate, gazing out at a colossal submarine stranded on the shore. The subdued, overcast sky and muted color palette create an atmosphere of quiet contemplation and melancholy. The massive, metallic vessel—out of place in this coastal setting—looms as a silent enigma, its presence both imposing and intriguing.

The man, dressed in a dark trench coat and hat, appears lost in thought, his posture reflecting resignation or perhaps wonder. A seagull gliding above adds a touch of life to the otherwise still scene, its flight contrasting with the submarine's immobility. The waves lap gently against the sand, reinforcing the tension between the natural world and this monumental, human-made object.

This piece invites viewers to reflect on themes of isolation, the passage of time, and humanity's relationship with technology and nature, evoking a poignant and open-ended narrative.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
This evocative artwork portrays a lone astronaut standing amidst a vast, undulating landscape of emerald-green hills. The setting exudes an ethereal tranquility, with rolling terrain stretching into the horizon, shrouded by a dense, foreboding sky. The dramatic contrast between the astronaut’s futuristic suit and the natural, almost alien scenery creates a sense of displacement, suggesting themes of exploration and humanity’s quest for connection with the unknown.

The swirling clouds overhead hint at an impending storm, heightening the tension in the scene and symbolizing the uncertainty and risks of venturing into uncharted realms. Despite the overwhelming vastness of the environment, the astronaut remains steadfast, embodying resilience and curiosity.

This piece invites viewers to reflect on the relationship between humanity and nature, the beauty of solitude, and the enduring spirit of discovery. It captures the delicate balance between awe and insignificance in the face of a mysterious, boundless world.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
This artwork captures a profound moment of human aspiration and cosmic exploration. A lone rocket ascends into the night sky, its fiery trail illuminating the desolate landscape below. The scene is framed by a barren, reflective terrain scattered with rugged rocks, their stillness contrasting with the dynamic energy of the launch. A thin pool of water mirrors the rocket's ascent, amplifying the sense of depth and infinity.

The muted tones of the sky and ground create a tranquil yet melancholic atmosphere, emphasizing the isolation of the environment and the singularity of the rocket's journey. The absence of visible life forms, apart from the faint silhouette of a solitary figure in the distance, evokes themes of solitude, determination, and humanity's relentless quest to transcend earthly boundaries.

This piece invites contemplation on the vastness of space, the fragility of existence, and the enduring human drive to explore beyond the known horizon.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
About the Artist
Michał Klimczak was born in Krakow, Poland. He is a self-taught enthusiast of photomontage and 3D graphics, and has been working with computer graphics since 2007. Klimczak has been collaborating with the Museum of Science Fiction on a wide variety of projects since 2014. 

In cooperation with the Pakistani photographer, Baber Afzal, Klimczak won 2nd place in the 2014 International Photo Awards competition in the architecture category and continues working to create fantastically surreal atmospheres.

A hardbound exhibition catalogue of Klimczak’s work is currently being planned will be published by the Museum of Science Fiction in 2023. The volume will include selections from Klimczak’s science fiction themed portfolio.

The world of Michał Klimczak is one of solitude and wonder. Figures in spacesuits stand alone beneath iridescent skies; hazy mists are draped over unrecognizable planets; massive spacecraft are crashed against rocky landscapes. His work shows us science fiction’s capacity to imagine new realities. The imagery that Klimczak uses explores possible futures, portraying dystopian scenes that still contain the hopes and dreams of humanity.

The artworks are marked by an intriguing use of texture, using contrasts in order to further enhance the overall boldness of these powerful works. The isolated figures are at odds with the grand scale of the landscapes, inviting a sense of both fear and hope. Where there is solitude, there is also the possibility of an unknown future. Although there may be alien structures in the sky, the grass on Earth is still soft and familiar.

Each piece presents a unique meshing between the familiar and the alien. Klimczak visually captures a wide range of moods, all while retaining a stunning vibrancy. He uses the imagery of science fiction to represent “alienating” ways of being in the world and to explore hopes and fears. Through this awe-inspiring exploration of our present emotions, this art may also shape our future.
The next release will include the following updates:

Meet Maria, the enigmatic and iconic character from Fritz Lang's groundbreaking 1927 science fiction masterpiece Metropolis. Maria embodies the heart and soul of the film, highlighting the struggle between human compassion and industrial tyranny and serving as a powerful symbol of hope and resilience in a dystopian world.

In Metropolis, Maria becomes a beacon of light for the workers who tire ceaselessly beneath the city’s massive machinery, advocating for unity and understanding between the privileged elite and the workers. Her role as a symbol of hope and unity is pivotal in the film. She becomes the inspiration for change, galvanizing those around her to rise above their circumstances.

The original robot Maria costume was created by sculptor Walter Schulze-Mittendorff and was worn by actress Brigitte Helm. It was created by first fabricating a full-body plaster cast around Helm, before then constructing the costume around it. Generous amounts of a substance originally used as wood filler allowed Schulze-Mittendorff to build a costume that would both appear metallic and allow a small amount of free movement, however, the costume was rigid and uncomfortable.

Despite this, Helm’s performance was exquisite, and Maria's character has left a lasting mark on popular culture. She has continued to captivate and inspire audiences with her hope and her resilience. Additionally, her image has become synonymous with the film itself, representing the enduring science fiction themes of social inequality, human nature, and the pursuit of a better world.
Creator: Thea von Harbou, Fritz Lang

Long_Description:
This piece draws the viewer into a narrative, inviting them to step into the picture and sparking their imagination. 
Although the crashed helicopter and bizarre backdrop may appear frightening or even sinister, there is a duality that deepens this artwork’s meaning and mystery. The strong, flat, deep blues are defined by delicate vestiges of light, illuminating the dark into lighter blues and soft oranges. There is a sense of hope and exploration, although it may be hidden in the shadows. Our  fragile hopes for the future bristle against disorienting glimpses into it.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description: Here, Klimczak creates an experimental composition, blending together imagery that is new with structures that are reminiscent of his other works. The science fiction symbolism suggests diverse modes of existence, and creates a unique artistic landscape. Here, we can examine present perspectives, as well as speculate on the future. The alternate realities that Klimczak portrays for us are both alarming and intriguing; as alienating as they are staggering. They serve as a reminder that, as humanity explores the cosmos, we must also grapple with what we find there.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
Missing invites viewers to contemplate the stark reality of climate change through the lens of a desolate landscape. With a broken-down brown car as its focal point, the painting serves as a visual metaphor for the consequences of human actions and the enduring strength of nature.

The abandoned car stands as a testament to human reliance on fossil fuels and unsustainable practices. Its rusty and weathered exterior symbolizes the outcome of short-sighted decisions that prioritize convenience over environmental stewardship. Placed against the backdrop of an arid desert, the car's presence becomes a stark reminder of the depletion of natural resources and the degradation of ecosystems.

In the distance, a gathering storm looms ominously, casting an atmospheric shadow over the scene. The approaching tempest mirrors the impending consequences of climate change, as intensified weather patterns and rising temperatures become increasingly prevalent. The storm also serves as a visual metaphor for the urgency of addressing these challenges before they reach a point of no return.

Through a muted color palette and intricate details, the artist captures the desolation of the environment and the tumultuous skies. The painting's composition compels viewers to consider the delicate balance between human progress and nature's equilibrium. As the storm gathers and the car remains stranded, a sense of urgency permeates the atmosphere, prompting contemplation of the steps necessary to mitigate climate change's impacts.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
To Create New Home offers a sobering exploration of the environmental repercussions of modern aviation in the context of climate change. Through a powerful image of a cracked jet split in half, resting in a desolate junkyard, the artist compels viewers to reflect on the ecological toll of human innovation.

The fractured jet, once emblematic of humanity's triumphs in technological advancement, is now a poignant representation of the complexities of progress. Positioned within a junkyard, it finds itself amidst a sea of discarded machinery, underscoring the transient nature of human achievements against the backdrop of a planet grappling with shifting climates.

This artwork starkly exposes the environmental consequences of aviation, a sector that contributes significantly to greenhouse gas emissions. The broken jet symbolizes the potential rupture between the sky and the Earth caused by these emissions, a poignant reminder that human activities come with an ecological cost. The composition prompts viewers to consider the ethical and moral dilemmas arising from such costs.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
New Day Under New Sky captivates the imagination by offering a glimpse into a potential future where humanity responds to the challenges of climate change with resilience and innovation. Through the portrayal of an astronaut exploring a new planet, grass underfoot and an ark-like ship on the horizon, the artist inspires contemplation of the possibilities that lie ahead.

The astronaut's presence symbolizes the pioneering spirit of human exploration and the potential for progress even in the face of adversity. Set against the backdrop of an uncharted world, the painting hints at the idea of seeking new frontiers as a response to the Earth's changing climate. The lush grass under the astronaut's feet serves as a testament to the regeneration of ecosystems, a hopeful sign that nature can rebound when given the chance.

The ark-like ship in the distance stands as a metaphor for humanity's capacity to adapt and survive. It echoes the idea of taking collective action to safeguard our planet's future. This ship represents a beacon of hope, embodying the commitment to resilience, sustainability, and intergenerational responsibility. The distant horizon invites viewers to contemplate the journey toward a harmonious relationship between humanity and the environment.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
New Day Under New Sky 2 transports us to another possible world where humanity may be more able to live in peace with its new planet’s environment. Through the serene depiction of a sun setting on a new planet, accompanied by water symbolizing the promise of new life and twin moons gracing the sky, the artist crafts a captivating narrative that prompts us to consider the transformative potential of confronting climate change.

The setting sun acts as a visual metaphor for the closing chapter of unsustainable practices and the dawn of a more sustainable era. The tranquil ambiance evoked by the painting's warm hues invites reflection on the beauty of nature and our interconnectedness with the planet. The sun's descent encapsulates the urgency of the moment, highlighting the limited time we have to embrace positive change.

The water featured prominently in the painting symbolizes the foundation of life and the potential for rejuvenation. It serves as a reminder of Earth's most precious resource, inviting contemplation of the importance of responsible water management in the context of climate change. The reflections on the water's surface mirror our collective responsibility to ensure the vitality of our environment.

The presence of two moons in the sky offers a subtle nod to the delicate balance of natural systems. This twin celestial arrangement prompts viewers to consider the intricate interplay of factors that sustain life on Earth and the need to protect and restore these systems. The moons' presence also encourages us to reflect on the duality of our role as both custodians and beneficiaries of our planet.

At the heart of the canvas, a radiant sun gracefully descends below the horizon of a new planet. Its warm, amber glow bathes the landscape in a gentle embrace, symbolizing the promise of renewal and the potential for new beginnings. The presence of water, glistening beneath the fading sun, hints at the essence of life and the planet's capacity to heal.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
New Day Under New Sky 3 challenges us to reflect on the interconnectedness of all life and the vital role each of us plays in nurturing our planet. It underscores the urgency of addressing climate change while also inspiring hope in the resilience of nature and the boundless potential for positive change.

New Day Under New Sky 3 offers a poignant exploration of the human role in the context of climate change, symbolized by an astronaut standing resolute on the rocky precipice of a jetty. Against the backdrop of a vibrant blue ocean and gathering storm clouds, the painting evokes contemplation of our responsibilities in safeguarding our planet's delicate balance.

The astronaut, clad in a vivid orange spacesuit, embodies the spirit of exploration and discovery that has driven humanity to new frontiers. Positioned at the edge of the jetty, they serve as a sentinel for the Earth's oceans, representing the collective effort required to protect our world's life-sustaining waters. Their presence also prompts reflection on the intricate relationship between human endeavors and the natural world.

The juxtaposition of the serene blue ocean and the encroaching storm clouds creates a sense of imminent tension. The vivid blue hues of the water symbolize the beauty and vitality of our oceans, while the approaching storm serves as a metaphor for the turbulence caused by climate change. This dynamic contrast invites viewers to consider the fragility of our ecosystems and the urgent need for action.
Through masterful brushwork and skillful use of light and shadow, the artist captures the ethereal atmosphere of a world in transition. The windswept storm clouds and churning waves mirror the tumultuous course of climate change, while the astronaut's determined stance highlights the power of individual and collective agency.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
The Return invites viewers to contemplate the intricate relationship between human existence and the urban environment in the context of change and decay. The painting captures a lone figure walking on railroad tracks, returning to a fog-shrouded city characterized by muted colors and dilapidated buildings, offering a poignant exploration of time, memory, and urban transformation.

The central figure, walking along the tracks, symbolizes the human journey through life's twists and turns, both physical and metaphorical. Their presence in the midst of this foggy cityscape speaks to the persistent human spirit navigating adversity. Positioned between two dilapidated buildings, they serve as a witness to the passage of time and the inevitable erosion of structures.

The fog that envelops the city and the muted color palette evoke a sense of nostalgia and transience. The city's architecture, once a testament to human endeavor, now stands in various states of disrepair. This portrayal prompts reflection on the fleeting nature of societal constructs and the impermanence of human achievements against the backdrop of changing environments.

Through meticulous attention to detail and play of light, the artist captures the quiet beauty of decay. The subtle interplay between the figure and their surroundings encourages viewers to consider the cyclical nature of renewal and decline and to reflect on the power of memory in shaping our perception of place.

The Return invites us to acknowledge the evolution of urban spaces and the stories they hold. It prompts discussions about urban planning, historical preservation, and the importance of fostering resilient communities in the face of change. As visitors stand before this artwork, they are invited to contemplate the layers of meaning embedded within urban landscapes and to find connections between personal narratives and collective histories.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
The Return 2 offers a haunting depiction of an abandoned cityscape, where a lone figure and their loyal canine companion traverse the aftermath of a recent rain. The painting serves as a poignant commentary on the effects of climate change and the intricate interplay between human presence and the evolving environment.

At the center of the canvas, a solitary figure walks alongside a steadfast dog. Their shared journey speaks to the enduring companionship between humans and animals, even amidst the challenges posed by a shifting world. The figure's presence amidst the ruins symbolizes the persistence of life, highlighting the power of human resilience against the backdrop of change.

The remnants of rain are evident in the glistening puddles and dampened surfaces, a transient reminder of the recent deluge. This detail underscores the unpredictable and intensified weather patterns characteristic of climate change. The abandoned city, once a bustling hub of human activity, stands as a testament to the vulnerability of urban environments to environmental shifts.

Through a carefully orchestrated play of light and shadow, the artist captures the atmosphere of a city in transition. The rich textures and subdued colors evoke a sense of desolation, prompting viewers to consider the consequences of unchecked environmental degradation on urban landscapes.

The Return 2 invites us to acknowledge the urgency of addressing climate change's impact on urban areas. It prompts discussions about sustainable urban planning, adaptive infrastructure, and the importance of community resilience. As visitors stand before this artwork, they are encouraged to reflect on their role in shaping the future of cities in the face of climatic uncertainty.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
Back to Zone presents a thought-provoking tableau that resonates with the indomitable human spirit and the enduring connections we forge amidst adversity. The painting portrays a figure clad in post-apocalyptic hazmat gear, warmly welcoming a dog amidst the ruins of a war-torn landscape. This evocative scene serves as a profound reflection on the impact of climate change and the profound bonds that persist even in the harshest of circumstances.

In the heart of the wreckage, the figure stands as a beacon of hope, attired in protective gear that hints at a world forever changed by environmental challenges. Despite the disarray, their posture exudes a sense of joy and compassion as they greet the dog, embodying the resilience that drives humans to overcome challenges and find solace in companionship.

The dog's recognition and response symbolize the unwavering connection between humans and animals, transcending the turmoil of a world in transition. The dog's loyalty and the figure's embrace evoke a shared journey through the tumultuous fallout of environmental degradation, highlighting the universal yearning for connection and support.

This painting serves as a powerful reminder of the strength of bonds and the potential for unity even in the aftermath of catastrophe. Back to Zone calls upon us to recognize the transformative potential of compassion and collaboration, inspiring us to forge a path toward a future where humanity and the planet can heal and thrive together.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
In the Forest invites us to view the interconnectedness of humanity and the environment through a vivid autumnal lens. The painting depicts an astronaut standing at the edge of a creek in a radiant orange forest, providing a poignant exploration of climate change's impact on our planet's delicate ecosystems.

At the heart of the composition stands the astronaut, a symbol of humanity's curiosity and capacity for exploration. Their presence in the vibrant forest invites contemplation of the juxtaposition between technological achievement and the natural world's intricate balance. The astronaut becomes an observer of the changing seasons, a witness to the interplay between Earth's climate and its seasonal rhythms.

The bright orange hues of the autumn leaves infuse the scene with warmth and energy, embodying the transient beauty of nature's cycles. The creek that winds through the forest reflects the importance of water in sustaining life, while the surrounding trees bear witness to the intricate relationships that form the foundation of Earth's ecosystems.

Through masterful brushwork and keen attention to detail, the artist captures the texture and brilliance of autumn's palette. The play of light and shadow accentuates the astronaut's awe as they stand before the creek, inviting viewers to experience the profound connection between humanity and the natural world.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
New Hope – wide invites viewers to embrace the spirit of optimism and renewal through the eyes of an astronaut gazing upon a landscape of majestic purple hills. This painting encapsulates the potential for positive change and the transformative power of collective action in the face of climate change.

The central figure, an astronaut, stands at the forefront of the canvas, their gaze fixed on the distant horizon. Their stance embodies the resilience of the human spirit and the capacity for growth and adaptation. The vibrant landscape of purple hills represents a future yet to be realized—a future that can be shaped through dedication, awareness, and sustainable practices.

The purple hues, infused with both energy and tranquility, symbolize the harmonious coexistence between humans and the natural world. This harmonious palette serves as a reminder of the beauty and diversity of our planet, and the astronaut's thoughtful gaze reflects a commitment to nurturing this delicate balance.

Through a deft blend of colors and subtle details, the artist captures the serenity and optimism that the scene radiates. The astronaut's exploration of this new terrain invites viewers to consider the potential for collaboration, innovation, and change as they gaze alongside them toward the horizon.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
Cursed Place offers a compelling narrative that delves into the evolving relationship between nature and humanity in the context of climate change. The painting portrays a faithful dog standing beneath the remnants of a stone archway, the guardian of a mysterious, fog-shrouded forest. This enigmatic scene invites viewers to reflect on the intertwining forces of the natural world and human impact.

At the heart of the composition stands the dog, a loyal guardian of the natural order. Positioned beneath the ruins of a stone archway, the dog becomes a symbol of continuity and connection, bridging the gap between the past and present. Their steadfast presence invites contemplation of our role as stewards of the environment, even as landscapes transform.

The cursed, foggy forest in the background reflects the darker consequences of unchecked human actions on the climate. The fog and muted colors evoke a sense of unease, underscoring the notion of an environment grappling with change. The stone archway serves as a portal into the past, reminding us of the stories hidden within landscapes scarred by time and progress.

Through nuanced brushwork and a play of light and shadow, the artist captures the atmospheric tension between the guardian dog and the misty forest. The interplay between the elements fosters an emotional connection that encourages viewers to explore the complexities of human-nature interactions.
Cursed Place encourages viewers to reflect on the importance of coexistence and symbiosis with nature.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
Dust portrays a gnarled old tree, its twisted branches bending over a distant figure in a rocky, dusty landscape. Bathed in yellow lighting overtones, the scene encapsulates the profound intersections of resilience, vulnerability, and hope.

At the center of the canvas stands the ancient tree, a silent witness to the passage of time and the evolving climate. Its gnarled form symbolizes both the struggles and fortitude of nature in the midst of changing conditions. The tree's protective gesture over the distant figure underscores the interconnectedness between humanity and the environment, revealing our shared vulnerability in the face of climate change.

The rocky, dusty landscape captures the arid reality of a changing climate. The yellow lighting overtones evoke a sense of intense heat and the relentless challenge of survival in harsh conditions. The painting serves as a visual reminder of the stark contrasts between life and vulnerability, as well as the delicate equilibrium that sustains ecosystems.

Through meticulous attention to detail and the skillful use of light and shadow, the artist creates an atmosphere that draws viewers into the narrative of resilience and adaptation. The composition invites contemplation of the intricate relationship between human actions, the environment, and the potential for renewal.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
Back to the City offers a striking portrayal of the intersection between human perseverance and the consequences of climate change. The painting features a solitary figure, adorned in protective gear, standing in an open space that was once a bustling city. Nearby, a stripped car and a discarded tire serve as poignant reminders of humanity's impact on the environment.

The central figure, enveloped in protective attire, becomes an emblem of humanity's adaptability in the face of environmental challenges. Positioned in the midst of an urban landscape transformed into a desolate expanse, their presence conveys a sense of determination amidst the aftermath. Their role as a guardian underscores the importance of facing the reality of climate change head-on.

The stripped car and tire embody the remnants of consumer culture and the implications of unsustainable practices. Their presence evokes a narrative of repurposing and reevaluation in the context of resource scarcity. These discarded remnants stand as tangible artifacts of a society confronting the repercussions of its actions.

Through the artist's adept use of color and texture, the painting captures the contrasts between human-made constructs and the raw elements of nature. The open space, once teeming with urban life, now exposes the earth beneath, revealing the layers of history and transformation.

Guardians of the Desolation urges viewers to reflect on the urgency of addressing climate change's impact on urban landscapes. It fosters discussions about sustainable urban planning, responsible consumption, and the role of individual and collective action in building a resilient future. As visitors stand before this artwork, they are invited to consider the choices that define our relationship with the environment.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description: Commonly incorrectly called bunkers, which are covered fortification structures intended for fire, observation, combat control, protection of soldiers and technical combat equipment against the impact of enemy means of destruction. They are usually built of reinforced concrete and, if necessary, also of other available materials such as bricks, earth, stones.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
Conclusion
Through the power of Klimczak’s art, visitors are compelled to confront the urgent reality of climate change. They are able to recognize that changes in one part of the world can have far-reaching repercussions on a global scale. As they leave the exhibit, they carry with them a profound understanding that art has the capacity to ignite awareness, inspire action, and transform the world, one artwork at a time.
Creator: Michal Klimczak

Long_Description:
The Togo Sofa is an iconic piece of furniture design, created by French designer Michel Ducaroy in 1973 for the French furniture manufacturer Ligne Roset. Renowned for its distinctive shape and unparalleled comfort, the Togo Sofa has become a symbol of 1970s design innovation, blending style with function in a way that has ensured its lasting popularity.

The Togo Sofa is characterized by its low, laid-back profile and its unique, quilted construction. Unlike traditional sofas with rigid frames, the Togo is entirely constructed from multiple densities of foam, giving it a soft, pliable structure that conforms to the body’s shape. This design results in an inviting, cozy seating experience, where comfort is prioritized without sacrificing style. The sofa’s form is inspired by folded cushions, with its signature pleated, creased appearance that creates a sense of casual elegance. The absence of hard edges or straight lines contributes to its relaxed aesthetic, making it a perfect fit for informal, modern interiors.

The Togo Sofa comes in various configurations, including armchairs, loveseats, and corner pieces, allowing for flexible arrangements to suit different spaces. It is upholstered in a wide range of fabrics and colors, from luxurious velvets to durable leathers, further enhancing its adaptability to various interior design styles. Its versatility and comfort have made it a favorite choice for homes, lounges, and even commercial spaces.

Since its introduction, the Togo has become a design classic, embodying the spirit of the 1970s with its emphasis on comfort, informality, and innovative use of materials. It has won numerous design awards and remains a best-seller for Ligne Roset. The sofa’s timeless design and enduring appeal have made it a beloved piece in the world of interior design, recognized not just for its aesthetic but for its ability to provide a uniquely comfortable seating experience.

The Togo Sofa’s legacy continues today as a beloved piece of furniture that offers both style and substance, perfectly encapsulating the era’s move towards more relaxed, user-friendly design principles. Its continued popularity is a testament to Michel Ducaroy’s visionary approach to modern furniture design.
Creator: Michel Ducaroy

Long_Description:
Djinn Chairs, designed by French industrial designer Olivier Mourgue in 1965, are iconic pieces of modernist furniture that became famous largely due to their prominent appearance in Stanley Kubrick’s 1968 science fiction classic, 2001: A Space Odyssey. These chairs are celebrated not only for their distinctive design but also for their role in defining the futuristic aesthetic of the mid-20th century.

The Djinn Chair is a low-slung, lounge chair characterized by its flowing, organic form that appears to hover just above the ground. The chair is made from a tubular steel frame, which is then covered with a thin layer of foam and upholstered in stretch fabric. This gives the Djinn Chair its distinctive, smooth curves and soft, inviting appearance. The chair’s seamless design, with no visible joints or separations, contributes to its futuristic look, evoking a sense of comfort and relaxation while maintaining a sleek, modern profile.

In 2001: A Space Odyssey, the Djinn Chairs were used to furnish the space station and the space hotel, creating an environment that was both futuristic and aesthetically pleasing. Their presence in the film helped to cement the chair’s status as an icon of 1960s design, symbolizing the era’s optimism about the future and the possibilities of space travel. The Djinn Chair’s association with the film has made it a symbol of modernism and forward-thinking design, capturing the imagination of both design enthusiasts and fans of science fiction.

The Djinn Chair’s influence extends beyond its appearance in Kubrick’s film. It represents the ethos of the 1960s, a time when designers were pushing the boundaries of traditional furniture design, exploring new materials, and embracing new forms. The chair is a perfect example of how design can transcend its functional role to become an integral part of cultural history.

Today, the Djinn Chair remains a highly sought-after piece for collectors and a staple in the study of mid-century modern design. Its timeless appeal lies in its ability to evoke a sense of the future, making it as relevant now as it was over half a century ago.

The Djinn chair is a piece of furniture in the Modernist style, created by French designer Olivier Mourgue in 1964. Originally called the "Low fireside chair," it is also commonly referred to as the "2001 chair," because of its prominent appearance in the film, 2001: A Space Odyssey.

The Djinn chair and a chaise lounge, was introduced by Airborne in Merignac Cedex, France. The rest of the series was released in 1965, and includes the iconic "Low fireside chair," a two-seat sofa, and a foot stool. The set remained in production until 1976.

The name "Djinn" refers to an Islamic spirit capable of changing shape. The design's low profile was an attempt to emulate the informal lifestyle of the time. The set was introduced during a time when growing interest in Eastern mysticism was influencing Western decorative arts.
Creator: Olivier Mourgue

Long_Description:
About the Artist
Michał Klimczak was born in Krakow, Poland. He is a self-taught enthusiast of photomontage and 3D graphics, and has been working with computer graphics since 2007. Klimczak has been collaborating with the Museum of Science Fiction on a wide variety of projects since 2014. 

In cooperation with the Pakistani photographer, Baber Afzal, Klimczak won 2nd place in the 2014 International Photo Awards competition in the architecture category and continues working to create fantastically surreal atmospheres.

A hardbound exhibition catalogue of Klimczak’s work is currently being planned will be published by the Museum of Science Fiction in 2023. The volume will include selections from Klimczak’s science fiction themed portfolio.

From: Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

Date: June 3, 2023

Welcome to the Museum of Science Fiction

About a hundred years ago Einstein said, “Imagination is everything. It is the preview of life's coming attractions.”

As a species, humanity is rapidly coming to an inflection point. We now hold the knowledge and the tools
to solve some of our most challenging problems and possibly, in the not so distant future, move towards a
post-scarcity civilization.

At the Museum of Science Fiction, art and science are powered by imagination. This simulation is designed
to preview life's coming attractions - both good and bad.

This project began as experiment to prototype ideas for a physical museum. The technology we used 
allowed us to go beyond experimentation and create a new center of gravity to educate and entertain people. 

Essentially, science fiction is the story of humanity: who we were, who we are, and who we dream to be.
We would like to go beyond 21st century story-telling and provide a space where people can expand
their imagination and consciousness of the real world.

"Reality is that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away" -  Philip k. Dick - Valis, 1981
Here, reality is a persistent environment that can provide inspiration to transcend reality.

We hope you will enjoy exploring the Museum and find inspiration to do something wonderful for humanity.

Greg Viggiano, PhD
Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

Introduction
Art that depicts the effects of climate change plays a crucial role in raising awareness, inspiring action, and fostering a deeper understanding of the environmental challenges our world faces. As our planet undergoes rapid and often devastating transformations due to climate change, the power of art becomes evident in conveying the urgency and complexity of this global issue.

In a world where the consequences of climate change are often overwhelming and difficult to address, art provides a platform for hope, reflection, and action. Its ability to convey the urgency of the issue, humanize its effects, and spark meaningful conversations makes it an invaluable tool in the fight against climate change. By encouraging us to confront the reality of our changing world, art empowers us to take steps towards a more sustainable and resilient future.

Themes
Ecosystem Disruption and Loss: Artworks under this theme depict the profound changes occurring within natural ecosystems due to climate change. From melting glaciers and rising sea levels to deforestation and dying coral reefs, Klimczak employs visual metaphors to capture the fragility of these environments.
Human Vulnerability and Adaptation: This theme focuses on the impact of climate change on human communities and in cities. 

Beauty and Loss: Here, artists convey the beauty of the natural world that is at risk of being lost forever. This theme invites viewers to connect emotionally with the environment, fostering a sense of stewardship and a desire to preserve the Earth's natural wonders.

Exhibit Goals
Emotional Impact: Art has the ability to evoke powerful emotions and create connections that facts and figures alone might struggle to achieve. When artists capture the tangible and emotional aspects of climate change, they can awaken empathy and concern in viewers, motivating them to engage with the issue on a personal level. This emotional resonance can drive individuals to take action, whether through lifestyle changes, supporting environmental organizations, or advocating for policy changes.

Visualization of Abstract Concepts: Climate change is often portrayed through data, graphs, and scientific jargon, which can be difficult for many people to grasp fully. Art has the potential to translate these abstract concepts into tangible, relatable visuals. By depicting rising sea levels, melting glaciers, extreme weather events, and their impacts on ecosystems and communities, artists make the consequences of climate change more comprehensible and immediate.

Catalyst for Conversations: Climate change can be a divisive topic, but art can serve as a neutral ground for initiating discussions. Artworks that showcase its effects can spark conversations among people with different viewpoints, helping bridge gaps in understanding and encouraging dialogue about the need for collective action. Art can create a safe space for conversations that might otherwise be difficult or uncomfortable.

The next release will include the following updates:
•        Personalized avatars
•        Selfie cameras for sharing pics on social media
•        Generative AI docents and tour guide avatars
•        Voice activated commands - like, "end program"
•        New galleries and exhibit areas with new display objects
•        Educational tours inside a quantum computer and a fantastic voyage to explore how neurons work
•        VR gift shop with MOSF merch!
•        Live programs you can attend with your friends

About the Artist
Michał Klimczak was born in Krakow, Poland. He is a self-taught enthusiast of photomontage and 3D graphics, and has been working with computer graphics since 2007. Klimczak has been collaborating with the Museum of Science Fiction on a wide variety of projects since 2014. 

In cooperation with the Pakistani photographer, Baber Afzal, Klimczak won 2nd place in the 2014 International Photo Awards competition in the architecture category and continues working to create fantastically surreal atmospheres.

A hardbound exhibition catalogue of Klimczak’s work is currently being planned will be published by the Museum of Science Fiction in 2023. The volume will include selections from Klimczak’s science fiction themed portfolio.

From: Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

Date: June 3, 2023

Welcome to the Museum of Science Fiction

About a hundred years ago Einstein said, “Imagination is everything. It is the preview of life's coming attractions.”

As a species, humanity is rapidly coming to an inflection point. We now hold the knowledge and the tools
to solve some of our most challenging problems and possibly, in the not so distant future, move towards a
post-scarcity civilization.

At the Museum of Science Fiction, art and science are powered by imagination. This simulation is designed
to preview life's coming attractions - both good and bad.

This project began as experiment to prototype ideas for a physical museum. The technology we used 
allowed us to go beyond experimentation and create a new center of gravity to educate and entertain people. 

Essentially, science fiction is the story of humanity: who we were, who we are, and who we dream to be.
We would like to go beyond 21st century story-telling and provide a space where people can expand
their imagination and consciousness of the real world.

"Reality is that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away" -  Philip k. Dick - Valis, 1981
Here, reality is a persistent environment that can provide inspiration to transcend reality.

We hope you will enjoy exploring the Museum and find inspiration to do something wonderful for humanity.

Greg Viggiano, PhD
Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction
