The Powerloader, officially known as the Caterpillar P-5000 Powered Work Loader, is one of the most iconic pieces of machinery in science fiction, immortalized in James Cameron’s 1986 film Aliens. This exoskeleton suit, designed for heavy industrial use, plays a pivotal role in one of the film’s most memorable scenes, where it is used not just as a tool but as a weapon in the hands of the film’s protagonist, Ellen Ripley (played by Sigourney Weaver).

The Powerloader is a mechanized exosuit that amplifies the user’s strength, allowing them to lift and maneuver extremely heavy objects with ease. Standing at over ten feet tall, the Powerloader is a formidable sight, with its large, hydraulic limbs and powerful claws designed for gripping and carrying industrial cargo. The operator stands inside the suit, controlling it with hand grips and foot pedals, effectively transforming their movements into the Powerloader’s actions. This design allows for precise and intuitive control, making it a versatile tool in various industrial settings, from loading cargo on starships to construction in hazardous environments.

In Aliens, the Powerloader transcends its utilitarian purpose when Ripley uses it in her final battle against the Xenomorph Queen. The scene where Ripley, clad in the Powerloader, faces off against the alien queen has become an iconic moment in film history. Ripley’s use of the Powerloader to protect herself and the young girl Newt is a powerful symbol of human ingenuity and resilience, emphasizing the theme of survival that runs throughout the Alien franchise.

The Powerloader is celebrated not just for its design and functionality but also for what it represents: the melding of human and machine to overcome overwhelming odds. Its rugged, industrial aesthetic has influenced countless designs in science fiction and remains a defining element of the Aliens universe, embodying the gritty, lived-in future that the franchise portrays.
Creator: James Cameron

Description: 3D Model from the film, Star Wars: A New Hope, 1977
Long_Description:
The Millennium Falcon, one of the most iconic spaceships in cinematic history, first appeared in Star Wars: Episode IV – A New Hope (1977), directed by George Lucas. Known as "the fastest hunk of junk in the galaxy," the Millennium Falcon is a Corellian YT-1300 light freighter that has become synonymous with the Rebel Alliance’s fight against the Galactic Empire.

Externally, the Millennium Falcon has a distinctive saucer-shaped hull with two forward-mounted mandibles and a cylindrical cockpit on the right side. Its asymmetrical design, combined with numerous external modifications and battle scars, gives the ship a rugged, lived-in look that contrasts sharply with the sleek, pristine ships of the Empire. The Falcon’s appearance reflects its history as a smuggler’s ship, heavily customized and often held together by the ingenuity of its owners.

The ship is most famously captained by Han Solo (Harrison Ford) and his Wookiee co-pilot, Chewbacca. Under their command, the Millennium Falcon becomes a key asset to the Rebel Alliance. Despite its seemingly battered exterior, the Falcon is equipped with advanced weaponry, powerful shield generators, and one of the fastest hyperdrives in the galaxy. This speed and agility are central to its reputation, allowing the ship to outrun Imperial forces and pull off daring maneuvers, such as the legendary Kessel Run, which Han Solo boasts of completing in less than twelve parsecs.

Inside, the Millennium Falcon is a mix of practical, utilitarian spaces and more personalized areas, like the lounge with its dejarik holographic game table. The ship’s interior is a testament to its long service and the various owners who have modified it over the years.

The Millennium Falcon is more than just a spacecraft; it is a symbol of rebellion, freedom, and the underdog spirit that defines the Star Wars saga. Its legendary status is cemented by its role in pivotal moments throughout the series, from the destruction of the Death Star to the battle against the First Order, making it a beloved icon of science fiction and popular culture.
Creator: George Lucas

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Lost in Space, 1965
Long_Description:
B9, also known simply as "The Robot," is a beloved character from the classic 1960s science fiction television series Lost in Space, created by Irwin Allen. First appearing in 1965, B9 quickly became an iconic figure in the sci-fi genre, known for his distinctive design, memorable catchphrases, and protective relationship with the Robinson family.

B9 is a fully articulated, humanoid robot with a bubble-shaped dome for a head, inside which is a rotating antenna and light sensors that serve as his eyes. His torso is barrel-shaped, with accordion-like, extendable arms that end in pincer claws, allowing him to perform a variety of tasks. The Robot’s body is supported by a treaded base, which provides mobility over the diverse terrains of alien planets. His chest is adorned with various control panels and lights, contributing to his futuristic appearance.

Although B9 was originally designed for space exploration, his role on the show goes far beyond that of a mere machine. He is equipped with a sophisticated artificial intelligence and often displays human-like emotions, including loyalty, concern, and even humor. B9 serves as both a protector and a companion to the Robinson family, particularly the young Will Robinson, with whom he shares a special bond. His most famous line, "Danger, Will Robinson!" has become a lasting catchphrase in popular culture, signifying his role as a guardian.

B9's character is also a reflection of the era’s fascination with robotics and artificial intelligence. While he is an advanced machine capable of complex calculations and analysis, his portrayal is warm and personable, countering the common trope of cold, unfeeling robots.

Over the years, B9 has remained an enduring symbol of the golden age of television science fiction. His design, personality, and role in Lost in Space have cemented him as one of the most iconic robots in pop culture history, embodying the themes of exploration, family, and the interplay between humans and technology.
Creator: Irwin Allen

Description: 3D Model from the film, Ex Machina, 2014
Long_Description:
Ava, the central character in Alex Garland’s 2014 film Ex Machina, is a groundbreaking representation of artificial intelligence in science fiction cinema. Played by Alicia Vikander, Ava is an advanced humanoid robot designed by reclusive tech billionaire Nathan Bateman (Oscar Isaac). She serves as the focal point of the film’s exploration of consciousness, ethics, and the blurred line between humanity and machine.

Ava’s design is a stunning blend of the mechanical and the organic. While her face, hands, and feet are covered with synthetic skin, revealing a lifelike appearance, the rest of her body is distinctly robotic, with transparent limbs and a visible, glowing neural network. This duality in her design symbolizes the tension between her human-like qualities and her artificial origins. Ava’s physical form is elegant and minimalist, emphasizing both her fragility and her advanced technological capabilities.

The plot of Ex Machina revolves around a Turing test, conducted by Caleb (Domhnall Gleeson), a programmer invited to Nathan’s isolated research facility. Caleb’s task is to determine whether Ava possesses true consciousness despite knowing she is a machine. As Caleb interacts with Ava, she demonstrates intelligence, emotion, and even manipulative tendencies, leading him—and the audience—to question the nature of her consciousness and intentions.

Ava’s character raises profound ethical questions about the creation of artificial life. She exhibits a range of emotions, from curiosity and affection to fear and desperation, blurring the line between programmed responses and genuine feelings. As the story unfolds, Ava’s quest for autonomy becomes the central theme, challenging the viewer to consider the moral implications of creating beings capable of independent thought and desire.

In the end, Ava transcends her role as an experimental subject, using her intelligence and charm to outmaneuver her creators and secure her freedom. Her journey from a controlled environment to the outside world leaves audiences contemplating the consequences of artificial intelligence that surpasses human control. Ava stands as a powerful symbol of the potential and peril of AI, making Ex Machina a modern classic in the sci-fi genre.
Creator: Alex Garland

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Star Trek: The Next Generation, 1987
Long_Description:
Over the course of seven seasons, Lieutenant Commander Data changed from a cold, calculating machine to an empathetic humanoid. Throughout the series, Data probes unceasingly into philosophical questions - going on trial to prove his sentience (“The Measure of a Man”), finding and losing his family (“Brothers”), and receiving a processing chip that allows him to experience emotion. Through this journey, Data grows into a character who is loyal, curious, and courageous, in addition to being synthetically constructed.

Commander Data, one of the most iconic characters from Star Trek: The Next Generation (TNG), is a fascinating exploration of artificial intelligence and what it means to be human. Portrayed by Brent Spiner, Data is a Soong-type android, designed and built by Dr. Noonien Soong. Serving as the second officer and chief operations officer aboard the USS Enterprise-D, Data is an integral member of the crew, known for his immense computational abilities, physical strength, and unyielding dedication to his duties.

Data’s appearance is distinctly non-human, with pale skin, yellow eyes, and a slight mechanical stiffness to his movements, marking him as different from his human crewmates. Despite these differences, Data is deeply respected and loved by his peers, not just for his capabilities but for his tireless quest to understand humanity. Throughout the series, Data’s central character arc revolves around his desire to become more human, a journey that touches on themes of identity, emotion, and the nature of consciousness.

Although Data lacks human emotions, his behavior is guided by an ethical subroutine, and he consistently demonstrates a strong moral compass. He is curious, logical, and often seeks to understand human emotions and relationships, even though he cannot fully experience them himself. This quest leads to some of the most poignant and thought-provoking moments in the series, as Data explores concepts like love, friendship, and mortality.

Data's relationships with other characters, particularly Captain Jean-Luc Picard and Chief Engineer Geordi La Forge, highlight his growth and the crew's acceptance of him as more than just a machine. His journey culminates in several key episodes, including "The Measure of a Man," where his sentience is legally recognized, and "The Offspring," where he creates his own child, further exploring the boundaries of AI and personhood.

Commander Data remains a beloved character in the Star Trek franchise, symbolizing the pursuit of self-improvement, the value of diversity, and the ongoing exploration of what it truly means to be human. His legacy continues to resonate with fans, making him one of the most enduring figures in science fiction.
Creator: Gene Roddenberry

Description: 3D Model from the film, Starship Troopers, 1997
Long_Description:
The Warrior Bug, one of the most fearsome and iconic creatures from Paul Verhoeven’s 1997 film Starship Troopers, is a terrifying representation of the relentless alien threat faced by humanity in this satirical military science fiction movie. Adapted from Robert A. Heinlein’s 1959 novel of the same name, the Warrior Bug is a member of the Arachnid species, a race of highly evolved, insect-like extraterrestrials with a singular purpose: to annihilate any threat to their hive-like civilization.

Physically, the Warrior Bug is a massive, multi-legged creature, standing over nine feet tall and weighing several tons. Its exoskeleton is a hardened, armored shell, resistant to most conventional weaponry, which makes it an incredibly durable and formidable opponent on the battlefield. The bug’s primary weapons are its powerful mandibles, capable of slicing through flesh and bone, and its razor-sharp legs, which it uses to impale or eviscerate its enemies. These creatures are designed purely for combat, with every aspect of their biology optimized for efficiency in war.

The Warrior Bugs are the primary shock troops of the Arachnid army, swarming in overwhelming numbers to overpower human soldiers through sheer force and brutality. Their hive mind-like coordination allows them to work together seamlessly, flanking and ambushing their opponents with terrifying precision. This relentless, almost mindless aggression makes the Warrior Bugs an embodiment of the film’s themes of dehumanization and the horrors of war.

In Starship Troopers, the Warrior Bug serves as both a literal and metaphorical enemy. On the surface, they are the monstrous antagonists that the human soldiers must fight to survive. However, their portrayal also serves as a critique of militarism and the desensitization to violence, as the human characters are pitted against a foe that reflects their own society’s descent into fascism and xenophobia.
The Warrior Bug’s design and its role in the film have made it a memorable and enduring symbol of the dangers of unchecked aggression, both in the fictional world of Starship Troopers and in the real-world parallels the film draws upon.
Creator: Robert Heinlein

Long_Description:
The Drone from the 2013 science fiction film Oblivion, directed by Joseph Kosinski, is a sleek, lethal, and highly advanced piece of technology that plays a crucial role in the film’s exploration of identity, memory, and the nature of warfare. Set in a post-apocalyptic future where Earth has been ravaged by a war with alien invaders, the Drones are autonomous machines designed to protect the planet’s remaining resources and eliminate any perceived threats.

The Drones in Oblivion are spherical, with a glossy white surface that gives them a distinctly futuristic appearance. They are equipped with multiple high-definition cameras, giving them a 360-degree field of vision, and their exterior is adorned with various weapon systems, including rapid-fire plasma cannons. These weapons make the Drones formidable opponents, capable of eliminating targets with pinpoint accuracy and devastating firepower. The Drones also possess high mobility, capable of flying at high speeds and maneuvering through complex environments with ease.

In the film, the protagonist, Jack Harper (played by Tom Cruise), works as a technician tasked with maintaining these Drones. As the story unfolds, the Drones’ true purpose becomes increasingly ambiguous, revealing deeper layers of control and deception. Initially perceived as protectors of humanity’s interests, the Drones are eventually shown to be enforcers of a much darker agenda, serving the mysterious alien intelligence that has manipulated Earth’s survivors into maintaining its control.

The Drones symbolize the dehumanizing aspects of advanced technology and the dangers of placing too much trust in machines. Their cold, calculating nature and relentless efficiency contrast sharply with Jack’s growing awareness of the truth, highlighting the film’s central themes of autonomy, trust, and the manipulation of reality. The Drones’ design and their role in Oblivion make them a memorable and chilling representation of the potential dangers of unchecked technological power.
Creator: Joseph Kosinski

Description: 3D Model from the film, The Day the Earth Stood Still, 1951
Fun_Fact: “Klaatu barada nikto” is the phrase that Gort needs to hear in case anything should happen to Klaatu. Although no official translation has ever been released, there have been many speculative interpretations, including: “I die, repair me, do not retaliate”, “Save Earth”, “There’s hope for Earth, if scientists can be reached”, and many more.
Creator: Robert Wise

Description: 3D Model from the film, 2001: A Space Odyssey, 1968
Fun_Fact: Director Stanley Kubrick had HAL 9000’s voice actor Douglas Rain sing the love song “Daisy Bell” almost 50 times in order to guarantee that the film’s most emotional and powerful scene would hit just the right note. In the end, he used the very first take.
Creator: Arthur C Clarke, Stanley Kubrick

Description: 3D Model from Star Trek: The Original Series, “The Ultimate Computer”, 1968
Fun_Fact: It’s in this episode that Kirk first quotes John Masefield’s 1878 poem Sea Fever: “All I ask is a tall ship and a star to steer her by”. Later, Kirk will use this quote again in Star Trek V: The Final Frontier. It is also referenced in Deep Space 9, as it is both paraphrased by Quark in the episode “Little Green Men”, and written on the starship Defiant’s dedication plaque.
Creator: Gene Roddenberry

Fun_Fact: It’s unclear when exactly Metropolis is meant to take place, as different versions of the story place it in either 2000, 2026, or 3000.
Creator: Thea von Harbou, Fritz Lang

Fun_Fact: The design for Wall-E’s eyes was inspired by a pair of binoculars, and his arms by inkjet printers.
Creator: Andrew Stanton

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Star Trek: The Original Series, 1966
Fun_Fact: Nomad can fire energy bolts at warp 15.
Creator: Gene Roddenberry

Description: 3D Model from the film, Forbidden Planet 1956
Fun_Fact: Forbidden Planet is loosely based on Shakespeare’s The Tempest. Shakespeare would definitely be fine with this, since Robbie is much cooler than Ariel (his closest counterpart in the play).
Creator: Allen Adler, Irving Block, Fred M Wilcox

Fun_Fact: At one point, Mike Medavoy, the co-founder of Orion Pictures, wanted O.J. Simpson to play the Terminator. In this version, Arnold Schwarzenegger would have been Kyle Reese.
Creator: James Cameron

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Lost in Space, 1965
Fun_Fact: Robot B9 was designed by Robert Kinoshita, who also designed Forbidden Planet’s robot Robby. The costume itself, worn by stuntman Bob May, weighed about 200 pounds.
Creator: Irwin Allen

Long_Description:
TARS, from Christopher Nolan’s 2014 film Interstellar, is one of the most memorable and distinctive artificial intelligences in science fiction cinema. As a robotic companion and vital crew member on the mission to find a new habitable planet for humanity, TARS is a prime example of how science fiction often explores the relationship between humans and technology.

Physically, TARS is far from the sleek, humanoid robots often depicted in films. Instead, he has a unique, modular design resembling a large, rectangular block that can split into smaller segments. This design allows him to adapt to various tasks, from navigating harsh environments to performing complex calculations. Despite his seemingly simple appearance, TARS is highly advanced, equipped with a range of features, including a humor setting that adds depth to his character and interactions with the human crew.

One of TARS’s key roles in the film is to assist the crew of the Endurance spacecraft on their perilous journey through a wormhole in search of a new home for humanity. He is not only responsible for handling technical operations but also provides emotional support to the crew, particularly to Cooper, played by Matthew McConaughey. TARS's personality is a blend of efficiency and wit, programmed to be both capable and relatable, a combination that endears him to both the crew and the audience.

TARS represents the theme of trust in technology—both its strengths and its limitations. As the crew faces numerous life-threatening challenges, TARS’s reliability and adaptability become crucial, highlighting the potential of AI to be both a powerful tool and a true companion in the most extreme circumstances.

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Star Trek: The Next Generation, 1987
Fun_Fact: Data’s cat Spot was played by six different cats, and an iguana (“Genesis”)
Creator: Gene Roddenberry

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Battlestar Galactica, 1978
Fun_Fact: While the original series ran for one year (with a few spinoff project), it's had a lasting impact on science fiction language. Battlestar Galactica introduced stand-in words to allow implied swearing and demonstrate an otherness to this humanity. Later shows such as Firefly, Farscape, and even The Big Bang Theory have used the same approach or made direct reference to this. Next time a character drops a well-placed "Frak!", you'll know who to thank.
Creator: Glen A. Larson

Description: 3D Model from the film, Ex Machina, 2014
Fun_Fact: In the scene where Caleb hacks into the security system to allow the doors to unlock, the code that is displayed on screen outputs the ISBN number for the book Embodiment and the Inner Life: Cognition and Consciousness by Dr Murray Shanahan. This is one of the main sources of inspiration for the film.
Creator: Alex Garland

Description: 3D Model from the film, Red Planet, 2000
Fun_Fact: At one point, one of the handheld information devices on-screen reads "HAB GPS GROK". The word "grok" is a Martian word used in Robert A. Heinlein's novel Stranger in a Strange Land.
Creator: Antony Hoffman

Fun_Fact: Out of every character in science fiction, Optimus Prime is probably the one with the most evil clones. The most noteworthy of these is Nemesis Prime, but other lesser-known clones include Darkside Optimus Prime, Toxitron, Scourge, and Gilthor (although he’s technically a hybrid between Optimus Prime and Megatron)
Creator: Takara Tomy, Hasbro

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Gigantor, 1963
Fun_Fact: There’s a life-sized, 60-foot-tall statue of Gigantor in his creator Mitsuteru Yokoyama’s hometown of Kobe, Japan.
Creator: Mitsuteru Yokoyama

Description: 3D Model from the film, Star Wars: A New Hope, 1977
Creator: George Lucas

Description: 3D Model from the film, Star Wars: A New Hope, 1977
Fun_Fact: R2-D2 has been used as an easter egg in many other films, including Indiana Jones: Raiders of the Lost Ark, Close Encounters of the Third Kind, Transformers 2: Revenge of the Fallen, and Star Trek Into Darkness.
Creator: George Lucas

Long_Description:
From: Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

Date: June 3, 2023

Welcome to the Museum of Science Fiction

About a hundred years ago Einstein said, “Imagination is everything. It is the preview of life's coming attractions.”

As a species, humanity is rapidly coming to an inflection point. We now hold the knowledge and the tools
to solve some of our most challenging problems and possibly, in the not so distant future, move towards a
post-scarcity civilization.

At the Museum of Science Fiction, art and science are powered by imagination. This simulation is designed
to preview life's coming attractions - both good and bad.

This project began as experiment to prototype ideas for a physical museum. The technology we used 
allowed us to go beyond experimentation and create a new center of gravity to educate and entertain people. 

Essentially, science fiction is the story of humanity: who we were, who we are, and who we dream to be.
We would like to go beyond 21st century story-telling and provide a space where people can expand
their imagination and consciousness of the real world.

"Reality is that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away" -  Philip k. Dick - Valis, 1981
Here, reality is a persistent environment that can provide inspiration to transcend reality.

We hope you will enjoy exploring the Museum and find inspiration to do something wonderful for humanity.

Greg Viggiano, PhD
Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

        Avatar configuration: right or left controller A button
        UI screen activation: point and click on object
        Movement: right or left controller joystick
        Fly: right or left controller joystick, point in the direction you want to fly
        Location bookmarks: right or left controller B button
        Communication: enable HMD microphone and HMD speakers
        
Desktop (Mac and PC) special features and functionality        
        Avatar configuration: press escape key
        UI screen activation: click on object
        Movement: W S A D and space bar keys
        Communication: unmute microphone and speaker(s)
        
Additional information        
        Some areas are restricted and only accessible by Museum members or staff

Terms of Service and Policies                                
        Notice of Fair Use                     https://www.museumofsciencefiction.org/ip1
        Privacy Policy                            https://www.museumofsciencefiction.org/privacy
        Terms of Service                        https://www.museumofsciencefiction.org/terms
        Code of Conduct                        https://www.museumofsciencefiction.org/code-of-coduct

The next release will include the following updates:
•        Personalized avatars
•        Selfie cameras for sharing pics on social media
•        Generative AI docents and tour guide avatars
•        Voice activated commands - like, "end program"
•        New galleries and exhibit areas with new display objects
•        Educational tours inside a quantum computer and a fantastic voyage to explore how neurons work
•        VR gift shop with MOSF merch!
•        Live programs you can attend with your friends

The Thumper is a simple yet crucial tool in the universe of Dune, originally depicted in Frank Herbert's seminal 1965 novel and later brought to life in various film and television adaptations, including Denis Villeneuve’s 2021 film. On the desert planet of Arrakis, also known as Dune, the Thumper plays a vital role in the survival strategies of its inhabitants, particularly the Fremen, the desert-dwelling people who have adapted to the harsh environment of the planet.

A Thumper is a cylindrical device designed to produce rhythmic, pounding vibrations when activated. These vibrations mimic the footsteps of large creatures, and on Arrakis, they are primarily used to attract the planet’s most feared predators—the colossal sandworms. Sandworms, or Shai-Hulud as they are revered by the Fremen, are immense, worm-like creatures that live beneath the sands of Arrakis, drawn to any rhythmic vibrations on the surface. This makes them both a deadly threat and a potential tool for those who understand how to control their movements.

In practice, Thumpers serve two main purposes. First, they are used as a decoy to lure sandworms away from the user, allowing them to traverse the desert without triggering a worm attack. Second, the Fremen use Thumpers in their daring practice of "worm riding," where they summon a sandworm and mount it to travel across the vast desert expanses of Arrakis. This practice is not only a means of transportation but also a rite of passage and a demonstration of mastery over the environment.

The Thumper's design reflects the Fremen's deep understanding of their world, blending simplicity with effectiveness. It symbolizes their ability to harness the dangers of Arrakis for their benefit, turning a lethal threat into a tool of survival. In the broader context of Dune, the Thumper exemplifies the theme of adaptation and the symbiotic relationship between the inhabitants of Arrakis and their harsh environment, underscoring the importance of understanding and respecting the natural world.
Creator: Frank Herbert

Long_Description:
The sandworms of Arrakis, also known as "Shai-Hulud" within the universe of Frank Herbert's "Dune," are colossal, iconic creatures that play a critical role in the ecology and economy of the desert planet. These titanic beings are not only central to the planet's ecosystem but are also deeply woven into the cultural and religious fabric of the planet's inhabitants, the Fremen.

Sandworms can grow to be hundreds of meters long, with thick, segmented bodies and a gaping, tooth-lined maw. They dwell beneath the vast sand dunes of Arrakis, surfacing to attack the rhythmic vibrations on the sand's surface, which they interpret as prey. This can include the large mining machinery used to extract the planet’s most valuable resource, the spice melange, which is found only on Arrakis. The spice is essential for space travel, making sandworms indirectly vital to interstellar commerce and power.

The life cycle of the sandworm is complex and unique. They begin as tiny larvae, known as sandtrout, which encapsulate water to form pockets beneath the desert surface, creating the planet's vital spice deposits. As they mature, these creatures undergo several transformations, ultimately becoming the giant sandworms. Their bodies produce the spice, and when they die, their decomposition leads to rich spice blowouts.

The Fremen, having adapted to life on Arrakis, revere the sandworms as sacred creatures and integral to their religion. They harvest the spice while also learning to coexist with the sandworms, even riding them as a rite of passage and demonstration of mastery over their harsh environment.

In the "Dune" series, sandworms symbolize the untamable nature of Arrakis itself and are a source of awe and spiritual significance. Their presence underlines themes of ecological interdependence and the consequences of environmental exploitation, serving as a stark reminder of the balance between harnessing and respecting nature.
Creator: Frank Herbert

Long_Description:
The Stargate is a central element in the "Stargate" franchise, which includes television series, films, and books. This fictional device functions as a teleportation portal, enabling instant travel between two distant points in space, often across galaxies. Its design and operation are pivotal to the narratives of the franchise, serving as a gateway to adventures on myriad alien worlds.

Physically, the Stargate is a large ring, typically made of a fictional, ancient metal that resembles stone but possesses unique properties. It stands about 22 feet in diameter and features intricate engravings and symbols along its circumference. These symbols represent different constellations and are used to dial specific destinations. The Stargate contains nine chevrons, each capable of locking onto a symbol to form an address that connects it to another Stargate, creating a stable wormhole through which characters can travel.

To operate, the Stargate spins its inner ring, selecting symbols in a sequence to lock in an address. Once the correct address is entered and the final chevron is locked, the gate activates, producing a watery-looking event horizon. Travelers can then pass through this event horizon to emerge at another Stargate located elsewhere in the universe. The process is both visually spectacular and central to the plot dynamics of the series.

The concept of the Stargate was first introduced in the 1994 film "Stargate," directed by Roland Emmerich. The technology is described as being created by an advanced alien race known as the Ancients, who used the gates to explore the galaxy and establish networks of connected worlds. This backstory enriches the franchise’s mythology and drives much of the series' exploration and conflict, as various factions seek to control or exploit the Stargate technology for their own purposes.

In the franchise, the Stargate symbolizes the limitless possibilities of human discovery and interaction with alien cultures. It is both a scientific marvel and a profound narrative device that facilitates the blend of ancient mythologies with futuristic science fiction.

Description: 3D Model from the film, Forbidden Planet 1956
Long_Description:
The C-57D is the iconic spaceship featured in the 1956 science fiction classic Forbidden Planet, directed by Fred M. Wilcox. As one of the first starships depicted in a major Hollywood film, the C-57D has become a symbol of mid-20th-century visions of space exploration, influencing the design of countless spacecraft in science fiction that followed.

The C-57D is a flying saucer, a design that was highly popular in the 1950s, reflecting both the era’s fascination with UFOs and the burgeoning space age. The ship’s sleek, disc-shaped form is both functional and futuristic, embodying the technological optimism of the time. Its smooth, metallic exterior, with a central dome and landing gear that retracts seamlessly into the hull, gives the C-57D a clean, aerodynamic look, suggesting advanced engineering and the ability to traverse the vast distances of interstellar space.

In Forbidden Planet, the C-57D is commanded by Captain J.J. Adams, played by Leslie Nielsen, and is tasked with a mission to investigate the silence from a distant colony on the planet Altair IV. The interior of the ship is just as advanced as its exterior, featuring a control room filled with blinking lights, sleek consoles, and a large circular viewport that offers a panoramic view of space. The design of the ship’s interior reflects the film’s vision of future technology—functional, yet with an aesthetic that emphasizes clean lines and a sense of order.

The C-57D is more than just a mode of transportation; it represents the pinnacle of human achievement in the film’s universe, a vessel capable of carrying its crew across the galaxy. Its arrival on Altair IV sets the stage for the film’s exploration of advanced civilizations, both human and alien, and the consequences of wielding immense technological power.

Forbidden Planet’s depiction of the C-57D helped establish the visual language of space travel in cinema, blending the era’s fascination with flying saucers and the anticipation of future space exploration. The C-57D remains an enduring symbol of the golden age of science fiction, representing humanity’s quest to explore the unknown and the potential dangers that lie beyond the stars.
Creator: Allen Adler, Irving Block, Fred M Wilcox

Long_Description:
The stillsuit is an essential piece of technology in Frank Herbert’s Dune, particularly for the inhabitants of the desert planet Arrakis. This specialized survival suit is a marvel of engineering, designed to protect its wearer from the extreme conditions of the planet’s arid environment, where water is the most precious resource.

A stillsuit is a full-body garment that covers the wearer from head to toe, with only the face exposed through a mask. It is made from a tight-fitting, multilayered fabric that is both breathable and durable, designed to minimize water loss through evaporation and perspiration. The suit’s most remarkable feature is its ability to capture, filter, and recycle the body’s moisture, including sweat, urine, and even exhaled breath. This recycled moisture is then stored in catchpockets and made available for drinking through a tube that the wearer can access without removing the suit. The efficiency of a stillsuit is such that it allows a person to survive for weeks in the harsh desert with minimal water intake.

For the Fremen, the native people of Arrakis, the stillsuit is more than just clothing; it is a symbol of their deep connection to their environment and their resourcefulness in adapting to one of the most hostile places in the universe. The Fremen’s mastery of stillsuit technology is a key factor in their ability to thrive in the desert and maintain their culture in the face of external pressures.

The stillsuit also serves as a narrative device in Dune, highlighting the theme of survival in a harsh environment and the importance of respecting and understanding one’s surroundings. It is a testament to the ingenuity required to live in balance with nature, a core message of Herbert’s novel. The stillsuit has become one of the most iconic pieces of science fiction technology, representing the blend of practicality and innovation that defines the world of Dune.
Creator: Frank Herbert

Description: 3D Model from the film, Planet of the Apes film, 1968
Long_Description:
The Icarus, also known as the Liberty 1, is the spacecraft central to the storyline of the original Planet of the Apes film, released in 1968 and directed by Franklin J. Schaffner. The ship carries the film's protagonist, Colonel George Taylor (played by Charlton Heston), and his crew on a deep space mission that unexpectedly leads them to a world dominated by intelligent apes.

Designed with the aesthetics of 1960s space exploration, the Icarus is a sleek, futuristic craft, reflecting the era’s optimism about space travel and the technological advancements of the time. The spacecraft has a streamlined, angular design, featuring a long, narrow body that tapers to a pointed nose. Its design is functional and minimalist, consistent with the portrayal of advanced, yet realistic, space technology in the film.

In the context of the story, the Icarus is equipped with a faster-than-light propulsion system intended for deep space exploration. The crew of the Icarus embarks on a mission to explore distant planets, with the goal of finding habitable worlds for humanity. However, due to a malfunction or unforeseen event, the ship veers off course, leading Taylor and his crew to crash-land on a mysterious planet, which they later discover is a future, dystopian Earth ruled by apes.

The Icarus’ crash landing is a pivotal moment in the film, setting the stage for the story’s exploration of themes such as evolution, societal decay, and the consequences of humanity’s actions. The Icarus is not just a vehicle for the characters; it represents humanity’s hubris and the unforeseen consequences of their quest for knowledge and power. The spacecraft’s name, evoking the myth of Icarus who flew too close to the sun, serves as a poignant metaphor for the downfall of humanity depicted in the Planet of the Apes series, making it an enduring symbol in the realm of science fiction.
Creator: Franklin J. Schaffner

Description: 3D Model from the film, Destination Moon, 1950
Long_Description:
The Luna 1 Rocketship, from the 1950 film Destination Moon, directed by Irving Pichel, is a pivotal piece of science fiction history and a symbol of the early optimism surrounding space exploration. As one of the first major Hollywood films to seriously depict a journey to the Moon, Destination Moon and its Luna 1 Rocketship set the stage for countless space adventure films that followed.

The Luna 1 Rocketship, also referred to as simply "Luna," is designed with a sleek, streamlined body typical of the era's vision of space travel. Its design draws inspiration from the German V-2 rocket, which was the most advanced rocket technology available at the time. The rocket features a tall, cylindrical fuselage with stabilizing fins at the base and a pointed nose cone, giving it a distinctly aerodynamic appearance. The Luna 1’s design reflects the 1950s belief in the feasibility of space travel, rooted in contemporary rocketry developments.

In Destination Moon, the Luna 1 Rocketship is the vehicle that carries four astronauts on humanity's first journey to the Moon. The film, which was produced with significant input from space experts like Robert A. Heinlein, who also co-wrote the screenplay, emphasizes the technical challenges of space travel. The depiction of the Luna 1 includes detailed sequences of its launch, orbital mechanics, and landing on the lunar surface, all of which were groundbreaking for audiences at the time.

The interior of the Luna 1 is depicted with a focus on functionality, featuring control panels, instrumentation, and living quarters designed to sustain the crew during their journey. The attention to detail in the rocket's design and operation was intended to present a scientifically plausible portrayal of space exploration, something that was relatively rare in science fiction films of that era.

The Luna 1 Rocketship is more than just a vehicle; it is a symbol of the boundless potential of human ingenuity and the belief in a future where space travel was not only possible but inevitable. Destination Moon and its Luna 1 Rocketship helped to ignite the imaginations of a generation, inspiring real-world efforts to reach the stars and cementing the film’s place as a cornerstone of the space exploration genre.
Creator: Irving Pichel

Object_Title: Flying Saucer from The Day the Earth Stood Still
Description: 3D Model from the film, The Day the Earth Stood Still, 1951
Long_Description:
The flying saucer from the 1951 film The Day the Earth Stood Still is one of the most iconic images in the history of science fiction cinema. Directed by Robert Wise, the film presents the saucer as the spacecraft of Klaatu, an alien visitor, and his powerful robot companion Gort, who come to Earth with a message of peace and a warning against humanity's violent tendencies in the atomic age.

The design of the saucer is sleek and futuristic, embodying the mid-20th century's fascination with UFOs and extraterrestrial life. The ship is a classic disc-shaped craft, smooth and metallic, with a seamless surface that exudes an otherworldly, advanced technology. When it lands in Washington, D.C., the saucer opens via a retractable ramp, allowing Klaatu and Gort to exit the ship. The simplicity and elegance of its design underscore the advanced nature of the alien civilization it represents, contrasting sharply with the more primitive technology of Earth.

In the context of the film, the flying saucer is not just a vehicle but a symbol of the potential consequences of human aggression and the possibility of extraterrestrial intervention. Its arrival causes widespread panic and fascination, reflecting the fears and hopes of the early Cold War era. The saucer's ability to hover silently and maneuver with precision reinforces its role as a harbinger of both awe and dread.

The flying saucer’s depiction in The Day the Earth Stood Still has had a lasting impact on popular culture, helping to define the visual language of UFOs and alien spacecraft in cinema. Its influence can be seen in countless films and television shows that followed, making it a quintessential representation of the flying saucer archetype in science fiction.

The saucer’s presence in the film serves as a powerful narrative device, emphasizing themes of peace, the dangers of nuclear proliferation, and the need for humanity to come together in the face of greater cosmic forces. It remains an enduring symbol of the era’s anxieties and the hope for a better, more enlightened future.
Creator: Robert Wise

Long_Description:
The Apollo Capsule, formally known as the Apollo Command Module (CM), is one of the most iconic and historically significant spacecraft ever developed by NASA. It served as the crewed portion of the Apollo missions, including the historic Apollo 11 mission, which successfully landed the first humans on the Moon in 1969. The Apollo Capsule symbolizes human ingenuity, perseverance, and the pinnacle of space exploration during the 20th century.

The Apollo Command Module was a cone-shaped vessel, designed to carry three astronauts into space and back to Earth safely. Its design was a product of extensive engineering to ensure maximum efficiency and safety in the harsh environment of space. The capsule measured about 11 feet tall and 13 feet in diameter at its base, with an interior volume of about 210 cubic feet, providing a compact yet functional living and working space for the astronauts during their missions.

The capsule's structure was composed of an outer heat shield to protect the crew from the intense heat of re-entry into Earth's atmosphere, which could reach temperatures of up to 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit. The heat shield was a critical innovation, utilizing ablative material that would burn away, absorbing and dissipating heat, ensuring the crew's safe return.

Inside the Apollo Capsule, the astronauts had access to various life-support systems, including oxygen supply, temperature control, and navigation systems. The capsule also housed the guidance and control systems necessary to execute complex maneuvers, such as docking with the Lunar Module and re-entry into Earth's atmosphere. The interior was cramped but carefully designed to accommodate the needs of the astronauts during their missions, which often lasted more than a week.

The Apollo Command Module was also equipped with advanced computer systems for its time, including the Apollo Guidance Computer, which played a crucial role in navigation and landing operations. Despite its simplicity by modern standards, this technology was revolutionary and instrumental in achieving the mission's goals.

The Apollo Capsule not only served as the living quarters and control center for the astronauts but also as their lifeboat, bringing them back to Earth after their historic journeys to the Moon. The image of the capsule splashing down in the ocean, with parachutes billowing above, has become an enduring symbol of the success of the Apollo program and humanity's ability to reach beyond our planet.

The Apollo Capsule’s legacy continues to inspire generations of engineers, scientists, and explorers, serving as a testament to what can be achieved when humanity dares to dream and pushes the boundaries of what is possible. It remains a symbol of human achievement and the spirit of exploration that drove the space race and continues to propel space exploration today.
Creator: NASA

Description: 3D Model from the video game, Mass Effect, 2007
Long_Description:
The M35 Mako is a prominent all-terrain infantry fighting vehicle featured in the popular video game franchise "Mass Effect." Designed by the Systems Alliance, a significant political entity in the game's universe, the Mako is engineered to support ground missions on various planets and environments encountered by players. It's named after the mako shark, reflecting its sleek design and versatility.

In "Mass Effect," the Mako is characterized by its robust armor and powerful weaponry, making it essential for exploration and combat on hostile alien worlds. Its primary armament is a 155mm mass accelerator cannon, capable of firing high-velocity shells, complemented by a coaxial machine gun for smaller targets. The vehicle's six-wheel drive system, combined with a low center of gravity, ensures stability and maneuverability across different terrains, from rocky cliffs to sandy plains.

One of the distinctive features of the Mako is its advanced suspension system, which allows each wheel to move independently. This enhances its ability to traverse rough landscapes and maintain speed without sacrificing comfort. Additionally, the Mako is equipped with thrusters that provide short bursts of increased speed or elevation, aiding in navigating obstacles or gaps in the terrain.
The interior of the Mako is designed for a crew of three: a driver, a gunner, and a commander, who also serves as a navigator. The vehicle also features a pressurized cabin, enabling the crew to operate in atmospheres that are otherwise inhospitable due to toxicity or lack of oxygen.

In gameplay, the Mako is not just a means of transport; it's a mobile base of operations that provides players with tactical advantages. Its durability allows players to engage in combat with enemy forces, explore uncharted territories, and complete critical missions. The Mako's integration into the game's narrative and mechanics demonstrates its integral role in the "Mass Effect" universe, symbolizing exploration and the adventurous spirit of the series.

Description: 3D Model from the film, The War of the Worlds, 1953
Long_Description:
The Martian Tripod is one of the most terrifying and iconic machines in science fiction, originating from H.G. Wells’s 1898 novel The War of the Worlds. These towering, three-legged war machines are the primary weapon of the Martian invaders, designed to wreak havoc on Earth and subjugate humanity. The Tripods have since become a symbol of alien invasion and technological dominance, featured prominently in various adaptations, including the 1953 and 2005 film versions of the story.

In H.G. Wells’s original novel, the Martian Tripods are described as colossal, metallic structures standing over a hundred feet tall, with long, spindly legs that allow them to stride effortlessly across the landscape. Their movement is both graceful and menacing, as they cover vast distances with each step, often outpacing the fleeing human population. The Tripods are equipped with several fearsome weapons, the most notable being the Heat-Ray, a devastating energy weapon that incinerates everything in its path, and the Black Smoke, a poisonous gas used to suffocate and kill any living beings in their vicinity.

The Tripods also possess long, flexible tentacles that can reach down and seize humans and objects, further emphasizing their otherworldly nature and the Martians' advanced technology. The sight of these massive machines, with their relentless assault on human cities, represents the overwhelming superiority of the alien invaders and the helplessness of humanity in the face of such an advanced foe.

In both the 1953 and 2005 film adaptations, the Tripods are brought to life with special effects that capture their terrifying presence. The 2005 adaptation, directed by Steven Spielberg, depicts the Tripods as massive, agile machines that emerge from beneath the ground, equipped with their signature Heat-Ray and emitting eerie foghorn-like sounds that heighten the sense of dread.

The Martian Tripods serve as a powerful metaphor for the dangers of unchecked technological power and the potential for destruction that comes with it. Their portrayal in The War of the Worlds has left an indelible mark on science fiction, influencing countless depictions of alien invasions and mechanical monstrosities in literature, film, and popular culture. The Tripods embody the fear of the unknown and the existential threat posed by an enemy that is both technologically superior and utterly indifferent to human life.
Creator: H.G. Wells

Long_Description:
From: Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

Date: June 3, 2023

Welcome to the Museum of Science Fiction

About a hundred years ago Einstein said, “Imagination is everything. It is the preview of life's coming attractions.”

As a species, humanity is rapidly coming to an inflection point. We now hold the knowledge and the tools
to solve some of our most challenging problems and possibly, in the not so distant future, move towards a
post-scarcity civilization.

At the Museum of Science Fiction, art and science are powered by imagination. This simulation is designed
to preview life's coming attractions - both good and bad.

This project began as experiment to prototype ideas for a physical museum. The technology we used 
allowed us to go beyond experimentation and create a new center of gravity to educate and entertain people. 

Essentially, science fiction is the story of humanity: who we were, who we are, and who we dream to be.
We would like to go beyond 21st century story-telling and provide a space where people can expand
their imagination and consciousness of the real world.

"Reality is that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away" -  Philip k. Dick - Valis, 1981
Here, reality is a persistent environment that can provide inspiration to transcend reality.

We hope you will enjoy exploring the Museum and find inspiration to do something wonderful for humanity.

Greg Viggiano, PhD
Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

        Avatar configuration: right or left controller A button
        UI screen activation: point and click on object
        Movement: right or left controller joystick
        Fly: right or left controller joystick, point in the direction you want to fly
        Location bookmarks: right or left controller B button
        Communication: enable HMD microphone and HMD speakers
        
Desktop (Mac and PC) special features and functionality        
        Avatar configuration: press escape key
        UI screen activation: click on object
        Movement: W S A D and space bar keys
        Communication: unmute microphone and speaker(s)

        Some areas are restricted and only accessible by Museum members or staff

Description: 3D Model from the video game, Mass Effect, 2007
Long_Description:
The M35 Mako is a prominent all-terrain infantry fighting vehicle featured in the popular video game franchise "Mass Effect." Designed by the Systems Alliance, a significant political entity in the game's universe, the Mako is engineered to support ground missions on various planets and environments encountered by players. It's named after the mako shark, reflecting its sleek design and versatility.

In "Mass Effect," the Mako is characterized by its robust armor and powerful weaponry, making it essential for exploration and combat on hostile alien worlds. The vehicle's six-wheel drive system, combined with a low center of gravity, ensures stability and maneuverability across different terrains, from rocky cliffs to sandy plains.

One of the distinctive features of the Mako is its advanced suspension system, which allows each wheel to move independently. This enhances its ability to traverse rough landscapes and maintain speed without sacrificing comfort. Additionally, the Mako is equipped with thrusters that provide short bursts of increased speed or elevation, aiding in navigating obstacles or gaps in the terrain.
The interior of the Mako is designed for a crew of three: a driver, a gunner, and a commander, who also serves as a navigator. The vehicle also features a pressurized cabin, enabling the crew to operate in atmospheres that are otherwise inhospitable due to toxicity or lack of oxygen.

Its durability allows players to engage in combat with enemy forces, explore uncharted territories, and complete critical missions. Description: 3D Model from the television series, Space: 1999, 1975
Long_Description: The Eagle Two Transporter is a spaceship used by Moonbase Alpha. This space shuttle for the Space TV series was designed by the Academy Award-winning SFX director Brian Johnson, who would go on to create special effects for Alien, The Empire Strikes Back, and more. This iconic ship was laid the visual groundwork for science fiction spacefaring vessels, and is an inspiration to so many other spacecraft designs.
Creator: Gerry Anderson

Description: 3D Model from the film, 2001: A Space Odyssey, 1968
Long_Description:
The EVA Pod from Stanley Kubrick’s 1968 film 2001: A Space Odyssey is one of the most iconic pieces of technology in science fiction, representing the film’s meticulous attention to detail and visionary depiction of space exploration. The Extra-Vehicular Activity (EVA) Pod is a small, maneuverable spacecraft used by the crew of the Discovery One to conduct repairs, retrieve objects, and perform tasks outside the main spacecraft while in space.

The design of the EVA Pod is sleek and functional, reflecting the film’s overall minimalist aesthetic. The Pod is a compact, spherical craft with a large, transparent viewport at the front, allowing the pilot a clear view of the surrounding space. The Pod is equipped with three mechanical arms extending from the front, each capable of performing delicate tasks, such as gripping tools or manipulating objects. These arms are controlled from within the Pod’s interior, where the pilot operates them using joysticks and controls.

Inside, the EVA Pod is designed for a single occupant. The pilot sits in a chair facing the control panel, surrounded by various switches, buttons, and displays. The interior is snug, emphasizing the Pod’s role as a short-range, task-oriented vehicle rather than a long-duration spacecraft. The controls are designed to be intuitive, allowing the pilot to precisely navigate the Pod in the vacuum of space.

In 2001: A Space Odyssey, the EVA Pod plays a crucial role in several key scenes, most notably when astronaut Dave Bowman (played by Keir Dullea) uses it to retrieve a malfunctioning communications antenna and later when he confronts the sentient computer HAL 9000. The Pod’s isolated, confined environment contrasts sharply with the vastness of space, heightening the tension and sense of vulnerability during these moments.

The EVA Pod symbolizes the advanced technology and the human ingenuity depicted in 2001: A Space Odyssey, but it also serves as a reminder of the fragility of human life in the vast, indifferent expanse of space. Its design has influenced countless other depictions of space vehicles in science fiction, making it a lasting icon of the genre. The EVA Pod represents the intersection of man and machine, a recurring theme in the film, and underscores the challenges and dangers of space exploration.
Creator: Arthur C Clarke, Stanley Kubrick

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Star Trek, 1966
Long_Description:
The Galileo VII is one of the most memorable spacecraft from the Star Trek franchise, specifically featured in the original series episode titled "The Galileo Seven," which first aired in 1967. The Galileo VII is a Federation shuttlecraft attached to the USS Enterprise, designed for short-range missions such as planetary exploration, personnel transport, and emergency evacuations. It represents the utility and versatility required for deep-space missions, embodying the practical and exploratory spirit of the Star Trek universe.

The shuttlecraft is compact, with a boxy, utilitarian design that contrasts with the sleek, more advanced look of the USS Enterprise. The Galileo VII is approximately 24 feet long, with a squat, rectangular body and stubby wings that house its warp nacelles. The ship is equipped with impulse engines for sublight travel and limited warp capability for short-distance faster-than-light journeys. The shuttle’s exterior is marked by the distinctive Starfleet insignia and its identifying numbers, making it immediately recognizable as part of the Enterprise fleet.

Inside, the Galileo VII is designed to accommodate up to seven crew members, with seating arranged along the sides of the cabin. The shuttle’s interior is functional, featuring a control console at the front with navigational and communication systems, allowing the pilot to manage the shuttle’s operations. The design is straightforward, emphasizing utility and efficiency, with storage compartments for equipment and supplies necessary for away missions.

In "The Galileo Seven," the shuttlecraft becomes the focal point of a dramatic survival story. Commanded by Mr. Spock (played by Leonard Nimoy), the Galileo VII is forced to make an emergency landing on the hostile planet Taurus II after being struck by an energy disturbance. The episode explores themes of leadership, logic versus emotion, and the challenges of command as Spock and his crew face dire circumstances while awaiting rescue.

The Galileo VII is more than just a vehicle; it is a symbol of the challenges of exploration and the dangers faced by Starfleet crews as they venture into unknown territories. Its role in the episode highlights the importance of teamwork, adaptability, and the balance between logic and compassion, core themes of the Star Trek series. The Galileo VII remains an iconic piece of Star Trek lore, representing the spirit of adventure and the resilience of those who seek out new worlds.
Creator: Gene Roddenberry

Description: 3D Model from the film, Fantastic Voyage, 1966
Long_Description:
The Proteus is an iconic fictional submarine from the 1966 science fiction film "Fantastic Voyage." In this imaginative movie, the Proteus is not just any submarine—it is an advanced vessel designed to be miniaturized to microscopic size. Its primary mission is to travel inside the human body to perform intricate medical procedures that are impossible at normal scale, highlighting a blend of medical drama with science fiction.

The vessel's design is sleek and futuristic, emphasizing its role as a cutting-edge technological marvel. It is equipped with various scientific and medical instruments that enable the crew to navigate and interact with the complex environment of the human body. The exterior of the Proteus features a transparent, durable material that allows the crew to observe their surroundings, including cells, blood vessels, and organs, up close.

Inside, the Proteus is staffed by a highly skilled team comprising scientists, doctors, and security personnel. This diverse crew ensures that the submarine can handle both the scientific challenges of navigating a human body and any unexpected threats that may arise during the mission. The interior is compact yet functional, designed to maximize efficiency in a constrained space.

A critical element of the Proteus is its ability to maintain precise control over its movements, crucial for maneuvering through the delicate internal environment of a human body. This is facilitated by its propulsion system, which allows for smooth and controlled navigation around blood cells, through veins, and past organs.
"Fantastic Voyage" and its depiction of the Proteus introduced audiences to a unique blend of exploration and medical intervention at a microscopic level. The film explores the idea of miniaturization technology as a tool for medical breakthroughs, making the Proteus a symbol of scientific possibility and the potential future of medicine. This vessel remains a memorable aspect of science fiction cinema, illustrating how technology might one day allow us to solve medical problems from within.
Creator: Michael Crichton

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Battlestar Galactica, 1978
Long_Description:
The Viper is the signature starfighter used by the human pilots in the television series "Battlestar Galactica," which has captivated audiences with its rich narrative of interstellar survival and conflict. This spacecraft is renowned for its crucial role in the defense of the human fleet against their robotic adversaries, the Cylons.
Designed for both space combat and atmospheric flight, the Viper is a sleek, single-seat fighter that combines advanced agility with deadly firepower. Its aerodynamic frame is highly maneuverable, allowing pilots to perform complex combat maneuvers during dogfights. The craft is typically equipped with kinetic energy weapons, such as autocannons, and has hardpoints for mounting missiles, making it a formidable opponent in battle.

The cockpit of the Viper is designed around its pilot, with controls that are ergonomically configured for quick responsiveness. The cockpit canopy provides a panoramic view, crucial for spatial orientation and target tracking in the vacuum of space. The Viper also features a sophisticated navigation and targeting system, which assists pilots in identifying and engaging threats.

Throughout the "Battlestar Galactica" series, the Viper evolves through several variants, each improving on the last in terms of speed, armament, and technology. The most iconic version, the Viper Mark II, is revered for its classic design and is primarily used by the series’ main characters. Despite newer models like the Mark VII offering enhanced capabilities, the Mark II remains a favorite for its reliability and nostalgic value.

In the narrative of "Battlestar Galactica," Vipers are not just military assets; they are symbols of human resilience and ingenuity. Pilots of these fighters are often portrayed as heroes, embodying the hope and fighting spirit of the human race as they navigate the challenges of their exodus through space. The Viper thus stands as a central element of the series, representing the ongoing struggle for survival against overwhelming odds.
Creator: Glen A. Larson

Description: 3D Model from the film, 2001: A Space Odyssey, 1968
Long_Description:
The Orion III Spaceplane is a fictional spacecraft featured in Stanley Kubrick's seminal 1968 film "2001: A Space Odyssey." As a part of the film's detailed and realistic portrayal of space travel, the Orion III serves as a commercial shuttle, transporting passengers and crew between Earth and space stations orbiting the planet.

Designed with a sleek, elongated body and large rectangular windows along its sides, the Orion III epitomizes the vision of future space travel as imagined in the late 1960s. Its aesthetic is both functional and stylish, reflecting a period when the excitement of the Space Age was at its peak. The design includes a prominent Pan Am logo, as the shuttle is operated by Pan American World Airways, suggesting a future where existing commercial airlines extend their operations into space travel.

The Orion III's capabilities are indicative of advanced propulsion technologies, featuring engines capable of supporting both atmospheric entry and exit, as well as sustained spaceflight. This allows it to operate efficiently as a transorbital shuttle. Its interior is reminiscent of an aircraft, designed to provide comfort and safety for space travelers, with seats arranged to accommodate the conditions of zero gravity and re-entry into Earth's atmosphere.

In "2001: A Space Odyssey," the Orion III is shown docking with a space station in a carefully choreographed sequence that emphasizes the elegance and precision of space travel. This scene is notable for its attention to detail and realistic depiction of orbital mechanics, showcasing the shuttle's smooth operation and the sophisticated infrastructure that supports space travel in the film's universe.

The Orion III Spaceplane is more than just a mode of transportation; it represents a bridge between the familiar world of commercial aviation and the frontier of space exploration. In Kubrick's vision, it symbolizes humanity's ambitions in space, blending the routine with the revolutionary in a way that captures the imagination and aspiration of the era.
Creator: Arthur C Clarke, Stanley Kubrick

Description: 3D Model from the film, 2001: A Space Odyssey, 1968
Long_Description:
The Moonbus is a fictional lunar surface vehicle from Stanley Kubrick’s iconic 1968 film "2001: A Space Odyssey." Designed to transport astronauts across the moon’s barren landscapes, the Moonbus reflects the film's vision of practical, near-future space exploration technology. It plays a critical role in the story, especially in scenes involving travel to a mysterious monolith discovered on the lunar surface.

The design of the Moonbus is utilitarian, emphasizing functionality over aesthetics. It has a boxy, robust appearance with large windows that provide panoramic views of the lunar environment. The vehicle is relatively compact, reflecting its purpose for short-range travel on the moon rather than deep space expeditions. It features a flat base with multiple thrusters that allow for smooth takeoffs, landings, and stable flight just above the moon’s surface, utilizing technology envisioned to be appropriate for the weak lunar gravity.

Equipped with a pressurized cabin, the Moonbus can comfortably accommodate a crew of astronauts in space suits, along with their equipment. The interior is sparsely furnished to maximize space for crew and cargo, illustrating the prioritization of efficiency and necessity in space vehicle design.

In "2001: A Space Odyssey," the Moonbus’s journey to the monolith site is a visually stunning sequence that showcases the stark beauty of the lunar landscape. The vehicle's ability to navigate this terrain highlights the advanced navigation and propulsion technologies expected to be developed by humanity’s future in space exploration.

Overall, the Moonbus is a symbol of human ingenuity and the extension of human presence in space. It represents a realistic approach to vehicle design for extraterrestrial environments, blending scientific feasibility with the dramatic elements of the film’s plot. Its inclusion in Kubrick’s narrative serves not only to advance the story but also to inspire thoughts on the potential directions of future lunar missions.
Creator: Arthur C Clarke, Stanley Kubrick

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Battlestar Galactica, 1978
Creator: Glen A. Larson

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Lost in Space, 1965
Long_Description:
The Chariot from the classic 1960s television series Lost in Space is a remarkable vehicle that embodies the spirit of adventure and exploration central to the show. Designed as the all-terrain transport for the Robinson family, the Chariot is a versatile, rugged machine that allows the stranded spacefarers to traverse the diverse and often hazardous landscapes of the alien worlds they encounter.

The Chariot is essentially a futuristic take on a snowcat, with a boxy, transparent canopy that provides visibility in all directions. Its design is both functional and practical, reflecting the show’s 1960s vision of space exploration. The vehicle rides on large, tank-like treads, giving it the ability to move over rough terrain, from rocky plains to snow-covered mountains. This mobility is crucial for the Robinsons as they navigate the many challenges of their extraterrestrial environment.

Inside, the Chariot is equipped with seats for the entire family, along with storage compartments for supplies and scientific equipment. The interior is designed to be a mobile base of operations, capable of supporting the family on extended expeditions away from their primary spaceship, the Jupiter 2. The Chariot’s dashboard is fitted with various control panels, communication devices, and even a Geiger counter, reflecting the show’s emphasis on the scientific and exploratory aspects of space travel.

The Chariot also features a retractable roof, which allows it to be easily loaded into the Jupiter 2 or to serve as a shelter when parked. Its transparent canopy, made from clear plexiglass, not only provides excellent visibility but also adds to the futuristic aesthetic of the vehicle, giving it a distinctive look that has become iconic among fans of the series.

Throughout Lost in Space, the Chariot is more than just a mode of transportation; it’s a symbol of the Robinson family’s resilience and resourcefulness. Whether navigating alien landscapes or escaping danger, the Chariot represents the ingenuity required to survive in the unknown. It has become an enduring symbol of the show’s legacy, embodying the optimistic spirit of 1960s space exploration and the adventurous heart of Lost in Space.
Creator: Irwin Allen

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Lost in Space, 1965
Long_Description:
The Jupiter 2 is the iconic spaceship from the classic 1960s television series Lost in Space, created by Irwin Allen. As the primary vessel for the Robinson family, the Jupiter 2 is central to the show’s narrative, representing the family’s home, their means of exploration, and a symbol of mid-century optimism about space travel.

The Jupiter 2 is a flying saucer-shaped spacecraft, reflecting the popular design aesthetic of the era. Its smooth, disc-like form is both futuristic and functional, designed to accommodate the Robinson family, their pilot, Major Don West, and the stowaway, Dr. Zachary Smith. The ship's exterior is sleek, with a central dome that houses the bridge and a lower section that contains the living quarters, engineering bay, and storage areas. The ship’s design is emblematic of the era’s fascination with UFOs and the concept of interplanetary travel.

Inside, the Jupiter 2 is divided into multiple decks. The upper deck features the control room, where the ship’s navigation and communication systems are located. This area is equipped with large viewing screens, control consoles, and various scientific instruments, all with a retro-futuristic design that reflects the show’s 1960s origins. The lower deck contains the living quarters, which include bunk beds, a galley, and other amenities necessary for long-term space travel, underscoring the idea that the Jupiter 2 is not just a ship, but a home for the Robinsons.

The ship is powered by a sophisticated, though fictional, atomic drive, which gives it the ability to travel great distances through space. However, due to sabotage by Dr. Smith, the Jupiter 2 becomes lost in an uncharted part of the galaxy, setting the stage for the show’s many adventures.

Throughout Lost in Space, the Jupiter 2 serves as a central hub for the family’s activities, whether they are exploring alien worlds, repairing damage, or fending off various threats. Its design, both inside and out, captures the optimistic view of space exploration that was prevalent in the 1960s—a time when the possibilities of space travel seemed limitless.

The Jupiter 2 remains one of the most recognizable spacecraft in science fiction history, embodying the adventurous spirit of Lost in Space and the era in which it was created. Its enduring legacy is a testament to the show’s impact on popular culture and the imaginations of generations of viewers.
Creator: Irwin Allen

Long_Description:
The Bubble Ship from the 2013 film Oblivion, directed by Joseph Kosinski, is a visually stunning and highly advanced vehicle that plays a central role in the film’s narrative. Piloted by the protagonist Jack Harper (played by Tom Cruise), the Bubble Ship is a sleek, futuristic aircraft designed for reconnaissance, combat, and transportation in a post-apocalyptic Earth.

The Bubble Ship’s design is a perfect blend of form and function, reflecting the film’s aesthetic of minimalist, high-tech design. The ship is characterized by its transparent, spherical cockpit—hence the name "Bubble Ship"—which provides the pilot with a 360-degree view of the surroundings. This panoramic visibility is crucial for navigating the desolate landscapes and ruins of Earth, as well as for engaging in high-speed aerial maneuvers. The cockpit is suspended in a gyroscopic frame, allowing it to remain stable and level while the rest of the ship rotates around it during flight.

Attached to the cockpit are two sleek, forward-swept wings that house the ship’s propulsion systems and weaponry. The wings can pivot and adjust, providing the Bubble Ship with incredible agility and speed, both in atmospheric flight and in the vertical takeoffs and landings typical of VTOL (Vertical Take-Off and Landing) aircraft. The ship’s design is heavily influenced by modern helicopter and jet aircraft, combined with a futuristic twist that gives it a unique and iconic appearance.

The Bubble Ship is equipped with advanced technology, including powerful thrusters for high-speed travel, laser weaponry for defense, and advanced navigation systems. It is also capable of docking with Jack’s home base, the Skytower, underscoring its role as an integral part of his daily missions to maintain and protect Earth’s remaining resources.

In Oblivion, the Bubble Ship is more than just a mode of transportation; it symbolizes Jack’s connection to the Earth he is tasked with overseeing and the duality of his existence—torn between his programmed duties and his latent memories of a different life. The ship’s sleek, functional design, coupled with its role in the story, makes it one of the most memorable vehicles in modern science fiction, reflecting the film’s themes of identity, memory, and the nature of humanity.
Creator: Joseph Kosinski

Long_Description:
The Ornithopter, often simply called "thopter," is a crucial and iconic vehicle in the universe of Dune, created by Frank Herbert. These versatile flying machines are an essential part of life on the desert planet Arrakis, where they are used for transportation, reconnaissance, and military operations. The Ornithopter’s unique design and functionality have made it one of the most memorable elements of Dune across its various adaptations, including Denis Villeneuve’s 2021 film.

Ornithopters are named for their method of flight, which mimics the flapping wings of birds or insects. Unlike traditional aircraft that rely on fixed wings or rotors, the Ornithopter’s wings beat rapidly to generate lift and propulsion, allowing it to maneuver with remarkable agility in the air. This design is particularly suited to the harsh and unpredictable winds of Arrakis, where stable flight is a necessity for survival.

Visually, the Ornithopter combines organic and mechanical aesthetics, often depicted with multiple sets of retractable wings that can fold away when the craft is stationary. The body of the thopter is streamlined and aerodynamic, built to withstand the rigors of desert travel. The cockpit is enclosed, providing pilots with protection from the extreme conditions of Arrakis while offering a clear view of the surroundings. The interior is equipped with advanced navigation systems and controls that allow precise handling, even in the most turbulent weather.

In Dune, Ornithopters are used by various factions, including House Atreides, for scouting missions, transporting personnel, and launching surprise attacks. Their ability to hover, take off, and land vertically makes them indispensable in a landscape where traditional runways are impractical. The thopters are also equipped with weapons, making them effective in combat scenarios, particularly in the frequent skirmishes over the valuable spice melange.

The Ornithopter is more than just a vehicle; it represents the ingenuity and adaptability required to survive on Arrakis. Its design reflects the blend of technology and nature that is a hallmark of Herbert’s world-building. The thopter’s role in Dune underscores the themes of survival, control, and the constant struggle between humanity and the harsh environment of the desert planet. It remains one of the most distinctive and enduring elements of the Dune franchise, symbolizing the complex interplay between man, machine, and nature in a world where all three must coexist.
Creator: Frank Herbert

Long_Description:
From: Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction

Date: June 3, 2023

Welcome to the Museum of Science Fiction

About a hundred years ago Einstein said, “Imagination is everything. It is the preview of life's coming attractions.”

As a species, humanity is rapidly coming to an inflection point. We now hold the knowledge and the tools
to solve some of our most challenging problems and possibly, in the not so distant future, move towards a
post-scarcity civilization.

At the Museum of Science Fiction, art and science are powered by imagination. This simulation is designed
to preview life's coming attractions - both good and bad.

This project began as experiment to prototype ideas for a physical museum. The technology we used 
allowed us to go beyond experimentation and create a new center of gravity to educate and entertain people. 

Essentially, science fiction is the story of humanity: who we were, who we are, and who we dream to be.
We would like to go beyond 21st century story-telling and provide a space where people can expand
their imagination and consciousness of the real world.

"Reality is that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away" -  Philip k. Dick - Valis, 1981
Here, reality is a persistent environment that can provide inspiration to transcend reality.

We hope you will enjoy exploring the Museum and find inspiration to do something wonderful for humanity.

Greg Viggiano, PhD
Executive Director, Museum of Science Fiction
