Description: 3D Model from the television series, Star Trek, 1966
Long_Description:
This Library Interface, an original prop replica from Star Trek: The Original Series episode “All Our Yesterdays,” is a remarkable representation of time travel technology in science fiction. In the episode, Captain Kirk, Spock, and Dr. McCoy discover a library on the doomed planet Sarpeidon. Operated by the enigmatic Mr. Atoz, the library functions as both an archive and a gateway, allowing individuals to travel into the planet’s past by stepping into recorded historical eras. As Sarpeidon’s sun approaches supernova, its citizens escape into carefully chosen moments of their history, raising profound questions about identity, survival, and the preservation of culture.

As part of the Museum of Science Fiction’s Time Travel Exhibition, the Library Interface illustrates the imaginative possibilities of combining knowledge and time manipulation. It explores the concept of time as both a refuge and a challenge, where history is no longer static but interactive. This device also reflects the ethical dilemmas of time travel, such as the consequences of altering events or abandoning the present for the past.

This artifact honors Star Trek’s legacy of blending futuristic ideas with moral exploration, inspiring audiences to consider how time travel might transform our understanding of history and our place in the universe.

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Doctor Who, 1963
Long_Description:
The TARDIS, short for "Time and Relative Dimension in Space," is the iconic time-traveling spacecraft from the long-running British science fiction series Doctor Who. First appearing in 1963, the TARDIS is one of the most recognizable symbols in pop culture, known for its unique appearance as a British police box and its extraordinary capabilities that defy the laws of time and space.

Externally, the TARDIS appears as a 1960s-style blue police box, a familiar sight in Britain at the time the series first aired. This appearance is due to its "chameleon circuit," which was designed to allow the TARDIS to blend into its surroundings. However, the circuit malfunctioned during the Doctor’s visit to 1960s London, leaving it permanently stuck in this form. Despite its unassuming exterior, the TARDIS is anything but ordinary. The police box exterior is an iconic part of its charm, and the phrase "bigger on the inside" has become synonymous with the show, reflecting the TARDIS’s incredible interior dimensions.

Inside, the TARDIS is a vast, complex, and ever-changing environment. The central control room, with its hexagonal console, is the heart of the ship, where the Doctor pilots the TARDIS through time and space. The interior design of the TARDIS varies depending on the Doctor’s incarnation, but it always maintains a futuristic and otherworldly aesthetic, filled with mysterious technology, pulsating lights, and intricate controls. The TARDIS also contains numerous rooms and corridors, including libraries, swimming pools, and even gardens, all within its seemingly infinite internal space.

The TARDIS is more than just a vehicle; it is a sentient, living entity with a deep connection to the Doctor. It has been the Doctor’s loyal companion through countless adventures, acting as a sanctuary, a home, and a powerful tool for exploring the universe. The TARDIS’s ability to travel to any point in time and space allows the Doctor to visit alien worlds, historical events, and distant futures, making it the ultimate symbol of adventure and possibility.

Throughout its many years on screen, the TARDIS has remained a beloved symbol of Doctor Who and has become an enduring emblem of British television. Its timeless design and the concept of infinite possibilities it represents continue to captivate audiences, making the TARDIS a central figure in the world of science fiction.

Description: 3D Model from the film, Back to the Future, 1985
Long_Description:
The DeLorean, specifically the DeLorean DMC-12, is an iconic sports car that became a cultural phenomenon largely due to its central role as a time machine in the Back to the Future film trilogy. Manufactured by the DeLorean Motor Company between 1981 and 1983, the car was designed by legendary automotive designer Giorgetto Giugiaro and engineered by Lotus. Although only about 9,000 units were ever produced, the DeLorean's unique design and its association with time travel have made it one of the most recognizable cars in pop culture.

The DeLorean’s most distinctive feature is its stainless steel body, which gives the car a futuristic, unpainted, metallic finish that sets it apart from other vehicles. This sleek, brushed steel exterior is complemented by the car's signature gull-wing doors, which open upward rather than outward, adding to its cutting-edge appearance. The car's low profile, sharp lines, and wedge-shaped nose are characteristic of the 1980s sports car aesthetic, emphasizing speed and modernity.

Underneath its stylish exterior, the DeLorean DMC-12 was powered by a rear-mounted V6 engine, offering modest performance. Despite its striking appearance, the car's initial reception was mixed, with criticism often directed at its underwhelming engine power and handling compared to other sports cars of the era. However, these critiques have been overshadowed by the car’s legendary status thanks to its role in the Back to the Future films.

In the Back to the Future series, the DeLorean is modified by the eccentric scientist Dr. Emmett Brown (played by Christopher Lloyd) to become a time machine capable of traveling through time when it reaches 88 miles per hour. Equipped with a "flux capacitor" and powered by plutonium (and later by a Mr. Fusion reactor), the time-traveling DeLorean became an enduring symbol of adventure, innovation, and the possibilities of technology.

The DeLorean's cultural impact extends far beyond its original production run. Today, it is celebrated as a symbol of 1980s nostalgia and science fiction, with fans of the Back to the Future franchise and car enthusiasts alike seeking out the vehicle as a collector's item. The DeLorean remains a beloved icon, synonymous with the thrill of time travel and the imaginative spirit of cinema.
Creator: Robert Zemeckis

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Time Tunnel, 1966
Long_Description:
The Time Tunnel is a groundbreaking artifact from the cult-classic television series The Time Tunnel. Created by Irwin Allen, the show depicted a top-secret government project that allowed scientists to travel through time via a massive, spiraling vortex. 

The futuristic design of the Time Tunnel, with its endless concentric rings and hypnotic glow, became an iconic visual representation of time travel in science fiction.
In the series, the Time Tunnel served as a gateway to pivotal moments in history, from ancient Troy to the future. It transported audiences across time and space, blending historical drama with speculative storytelling. At its core, the Time Tunnel explored humanity’s fascination with time travel, the consequences of altering history, and the ethical dilemmas of such technology.

This artifact holds a special place in the Museum of Science Fiction as a symbol of 1960s sci-fi imagination and ingenuity. Its design reflected the era's optimism about technology’s potential to unlock the mysteries of the universe. By showcasing the Time Tunnel, the museum honors the enduring appeal of time travel as a narrative device and its role in inspiring scientific curiosity and creativity across generations.

Description: 3D Model from the book, A Sound of Thunder, 1952
Long_Description:
Ray Bradbury’s “A Sound of Thunder” is a seminal work in the exploration of time travel and its profound implications. The story, first published in 1952, follows a group of travelers who journey back to the prehistoric past on a safari to hunt dinosaurs. A seemingly minor act—stepping on a butterfly—creates a cascading effect that drastically alters the present, illustrating the now-famous “butterfly effect.”

In the Time Travel Exhibition, this story stands out as a foundational exploration of how small actions in the past can have unforeseen and monumental consequences. Bradbury’s vivid narrative and compelling premise popularized the idea of time travel as more than just an adventure—it is a delicate and potentially dangerous endeavor, highlighting humanity’s responsibility when meddling with time.

“A Sound of Thunder” resonates with the broader themes of the exhibition: the ethical dilemmas, scientific paradoxes, and awe-inspiring possibilities of time travel. Its influence extends beyond literature, shaping the portrayal of time travel in film, television, and popular culture.

This exhibit celebrates Bradbury’s visionary imagination, showcasing how his work continues to inspire curiosity and caution about the profound interconnectedness of time, space, and human actions.

Description: 3D Model from the film, The Time Machine, 1960
Long_Description:
The Time Machine, featured in the 1960 film adaptation of H.G. Wells’ classic 1895 novel, is a masterpiece of science fiction design and storytelling. Directed by George Pal, the film brought Wells’ visionary tale to life, introducing audiences to a steampunk-inspired machine that could travel across centuries. With its rotating disc, ornate levers, and artful Victorian aesthetic, the Time Machine became an enduring symbol of humanity’s fascination with conquering time.

In the Time Travel Exhibition, the Time Machine represents the genesis of modern time travel narratives. It explores themes central to the exhibition: humanity’s desire to understand and manipulate time, the consequences of tampering with the past or future, and the moral questions that arise from such power. The machine is not merely a tool for adventure; it is a vehicle for exploring profound questions about progress, decay, and the human condition.

This exhibit celebrates the Time Machine’s cultural and artistic legacy. Its influence on science fiction is immeasurable, inspiring countless films, novels, and series. By showcasing this artifact, the Museum of Science Fiction honors the story that defined time travel as a genre and continues to spark curiosity about the possibilities of temporal exploration.

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Star Trek, 1966
Long_Description:
In Star Trek: The Original Series episode "The Alternative Factor" (Season 1, Episode 27), a small, domed timeship plays a pivotal role in the conflict between two versions of Lazarus—one from the matter universe and the other from an antimatter universe. This single-person spacecraft, powered by dilithium crystals, enables travel through space-time and grants access to the negative magnetic corridor, a dimension that separates the two universes.

The episode begins when the USS Enterprise detects a powerful anomaly that briefly causes the entire universe to "blink" out of existence. The disturbance is linked to Lazarus, a seemingly deranged man who claims to be hunting a deadly enemy. As Captain Kirk and his crew investigate, they uncover the truth: there are two versions of Lazarus, one from each universe. If they were to meet in the same dimension, their contact would annihilate both realities.

The timeship serves as the gateway between these universes, allowing Lazarus to travel between them via the negative magnetic corridor. However, his relentless pursuit of his counterpart destabilizes space-time, threatening all of existence. Realizing the stakes, Kirk takes decisive action—he ensures that both versions of Lazarus are trapped inside the corridor, sealing them away forever to prevent their destructive collision.

This episode explores profound themes of duality, sacrifice, and the perils of unrestrained scientific ambition. While the timeship represents advanced technology, it ultimately becomes an instrument of eternal imprisonment, symbolizing the delicate balance between opposing forces in the universe.

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Star Trek, 1966
Long_Description:
The Guardian of Forever, featured in the Star Trek: The Original Series episode "The City on the Edge of Forever," is one of the most iconic representations of time travel in science fiction. A mysterious, ancient structure, the Guardian is a portal through time, allowing individuals to step into any moment in history. Its glowing, circular opening acts as a gateway to the past and future, demonstrating the immense possibilities and dangers of manipulating time.

In Star Trek, the Guardian of Forever is not just a device for travel, but a profound force that enforces the stability of time. When the crew of the U.S.S. Enterprise uses the Guardian, they must confront the consequences of their actions, reinforcing the series’ recurring themes of moral responsibility and the impact of decisions across time.

As part of the Museum of Science Fiction’s Time Travel Exhibition, the Guardian of Forever stands as a symbol of the genre’s exploration of temporal paradoxes and ethical dilemmas. It challenges audiences to consider how time travel might not only change the past and future but also shape the present.

This artifact celebrates Star Trek’s lasting legacy in time travel narratives, inviting visitors to explore the complexity and wonder of time manipulation in speculative fiction.

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Star Trek, 1966
Long_Description:
"Tomorrow Is Yesterday," a pivotal episode from Star Trek: The Original Series, explores the complexities of time travel through the accidental journey of the U.S.S. Enterprise back to the 20th century. In this episode, the crew finds themselves in 1969, just as a military pilot, Captain John Christopher, is about to intercept the starship. The episode delves into the consequences of interacting with past events and the delicate balance required to preserve the timeline.

In the Time Travel Exhibition, this episode is significant for its portrayal of the ethical and practical challenges of time travel. The Enterprise crew is forced to correct their inadvertent changes to the past, reflecting one of Star Trek's central themes: the responsibility that comes with technological advancement and the need for moral restraint. The time travel narrative also highlights the idea that even small actions in the past can have far-reaching impacts on the future.

By featuring "Tomorrow Is Yesterday", the Museum of Science Fiction showcases how Star Trek challenged audiences to think about time travel not just as an adventure, but as a complex force that could shape both history and the future. The episode remains a touchstone for exploring the paradoxes and consequences inherent in time travel.

The Ranger, a versatile spacecraft featured in Christopher Nolan’s Interstellar, is a stunning example of futuristic design grounded in real-world science. Developed to navigate both space and planetary atmospheres, the Ranger played a vital role in the film’s exploration of humanity’s quest for survival beyond Earth. Its sleek, aerodynamic design allowed it to efficiently enter and exit planetary gravity wells, while its modular capabilities made it essential for transporting crew and cargo during interstellar missions.

In Interstellar, the Ranger symbolizes humanity’s ingenuity and resilience in the face of extinction. It represents not just a means of transportation, but a lifeline to hope and survival. The film’s production team worked closely with scientific advisors, including physicist Kip Thorne, to ensure the ship’s design and functionality adhered to the laws of physics, making the Ranger a blend of cinematic spectacle and scientific credibility.

As part of the Museum of Science Fiction, the Ranger serves as a bridge between science fiction and real-world space exploration. It highlights the interplay between speculative storytelling and technological inspiration, showing how science fiction envisions humanity’s potential to explore and adapt to the cosmos. The Ranger inspires visitors to dream of a future where interstellar travel becomes a reality.

Description: 3D Model from the television series, Terra Nova, 2011
Long_Description:
Terra Nova, a science fiction television series that premiered in 2011, takes audiences on an ambitious journey into both the past and the future. Set in the year 2149, humanity is on the brink of collapse due to environmental degradation. To save the future, scientists discover a time portal that allows people to travel back 85 million years to the prehistoric Earth, where they establish a new colony. This premise blends time travel with the exploration of past civilizations, offering a unique take on the genre.

In the Time Travel Exhibition, Terra Nova offers a fresh perspective on the concept of time travel by exploring the possibility of using the past—not just as a destination for adventure, but as a way to ensure humanity's survival. The series also poses ethical questions about altering history, as well as the consequences of reintroducing humans into a world of dinosaurs.

This exhibit highlights Terra Nova’s contribution to science fiction’s portrayal of time travel, bridging the gap between speculative technology and ecological caution. By revisiting Earth’s prehistoric past, the show explores how science fiction can examine the implications of our present actions on future generations. Terra Nova challenges audiences to think about the responsibilities and risks of manipulating time for survival.

Invasive Species Report Xenomorph: Stage 2
Scientific Name Not yet Classified.
Regulatory Classification Highly Prohibited Species.
Organism Distinguishing Features
The Stage 2 organism has eight long, finger-like legs, which allow it to crawl rapidly, and a long tail adapted for making great leaps. These particular appendages give it an appearance somewhat comparable to chelicerate arthropods such as arachnids and horseshoe crabs.

The Stage 2 organism is a parasitoid; its only purpose is to make contact with the host's mouth for the implantation process, by gripping its legs around the host's head and wrapping its tail around the host's neck. Upon making contact, the Stage 2 organism administers a cynose-based paralytic in order to render the host unconscious. During a successful attachment, the Stage 2 organism will insert a proboscis down the host's throat, supplying it with oxygen while simultaneously implanting an embryo. Attempts to remove the Stage 2 organism generally prove fatal, as the parasitoid will respond by tightening its tail around the host's neck, and its acidic blood prevents it from being safely cut away. In addition, the organism grip on the host's head is strong enough to damage the host's body if it is forcibly removed. Once the Stage 2 embryo is safely implanted, the Stage 2 organism detaches, crawls away and dies.

Organism Origin Unknown.

Organism Habitat and Growth
The species is eusocial with a caste system ruled over by a queen. Their life cycle comprises three distinct stages: they begin Stage 1 life as an egg, which hatches a Stage 2 parasitoid larval form. The Stage 2 organism attaches itself to a living host by, latching onto its face.  The Stage 2 organism then "impregnates" the host with an embryo which, after a period of gestation, erupts violently from the host's chest, resulting in the death of the host.

The Stage 3 organism then matures to an adult phase, shedding its skin and replacing its cells with polarized silicon. Due to horizontal gene transfer during the gestation period, the organism also takes on some of the basic physical attributes of the host from which it was born, allowing the individual organism to adapt to the host's environment.

The Stage 3 adult organism is bipedal in form, though depending on their host species, they will either adopt a more hunched stance, or remain quadrupedal when walking or sprinting. Their overall stance and general behavior is a result of the mixture of the genetic DNA of the embryo and the host. The organism has a skeletal, biomechanical appearance and are usually colored in muted shades of black, gray, or bronze. Their body heat matches the ambient temperature of the environment in which they are found, so they do not radiate heat, making them indistinguishable from their surroundings through thermal imaging. Adult organisms are capable of running and crawling along ceilings and walls. They have great physical strength. The adult organism has a segmented, blade-tipped tails and elongated, cylindrical skulls, but possess no visible eyes. The organism has been found to be both plantigrade and digitigrade, usually relative to their host. 

Organism Reproduction 
Organism queens are significantly larger and stronger than normal adults, being approximately 4.5 metres (15 ft) tall. Their body structure differs also, having two pairs of arms, one large and one small. The queen's head is larger than those of other adult organisms, and is protected by a large, flat crest, like a crown, which varies from queen to queen. 

Egg-laying queens possess an immense ovipositor attached to their lower torso, similar to a queen termite. Unlike insect queens, there appears to be no need for an Alien queen's eggs to be fertilized. When attached to her ovipositor, the queen is supported by a "biomechanical throne" that consists of a lattice of struts resembling massive insect legs.

Environmental Impacts
Xenomorph organisms pose maximum and multiple possible threats to human life. The organism’s blood is an extremely potent acid and is capable of corroding almost any substance on contact with alarming speed. It is dull yellowish-green in color and appears to be pressurized inside the body so that it spurts out with great force when the organism is cut. It is theorized that the acid blood could be some type of "hydrosulphuric acid" compound due to its corrosiveness and the conspicuously toxic effects on human tissue. 

Organisms seem to be immune to their own acidic and toxic liquids due to an endobiological build-up, similar to the human stomach's ability to protect itself from its own digestive fluids. 

Organisms can produce a thick, strong resin that they use to build their hives and to cocoon their hosts. Organisms also use the resin to camouflage the walls of their hives. Organisms salivate heavily in the form of a sticky, clear fluid; while presently known to be a nontoxic substance.

Similar Native Species
None.

Management 
Undetermined at this time.

Location: Special Exhibit Area 4: Future of Energy
